title: Catalog binary: 0x10 flag bit semantics + inferBinaries pairing semantics
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

Two distinct issues in scripts/build-catalog.ts and scripts/catalog-pure.ts:

**A. 0x10 flag bit semantics diverge between two writers**
- `applyDoublesFlag` (build-catalog.ts:367) sets `0x10` on every star whose HIP appears in the CCDM doubles set — applies to both components.
- `inferBinaries` (catalog-pure.ts:287) sets `0x10` only on the brighter member of a geometric pair.

The renderer reads bit `0x10` with one assumed meaning, but receives a mix. Either align both writers to the same semantics (mark all components, or only primaries) or split into two bits.

**B. inferBinaries produces a directed nearest-neighbour graph, not mutual pairs**
- catalog-pure.ts:269-282 finds each star's nearest neighbour and assigns it as `companionIdx` without checking that the relationship is mutual. The header comment (line 226-229) implies pair semantics ("record as this star's companion and flag the brighter of the pair").
- Result: A→B and B→C is possible. The "primary" pick at line 287 is per-i and overlaps incoherently in 3-star clusters.

Fix: Decide between mutual-only pairs (post-process to require A→B ∧ B→A) or document the directed-graph behaviour explicitly. Either way, align the renderer's interpretation.

Severity: CRITICAL — the binary feeds every client.
notes: Fix landed in PR #13 (claude/fix-binary-flag-semantics). Both writers now treat 0x10 as 'primary anchor of a system, at most one per system': inferBinaries flags only the brighter member of mutual pairs (companionIdx remains directed for renderer use), applyDoublesFlag groups CCDM HIPs by CCDM_ID and picks brightest per group. KNOWN_VISUAL_DOUBLES restructured into {components, reason} systems so 61 Cyg A+B share one group. Tests updated; rebuild stats: 14 mutual geometric pairs + 12384 new CCDM primaries on top, 9-system overlap shows agreement between writers.

title: GPU resource disposal audit — Stellata.dispose() leaks materials/geometries
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

`Stellata.dispose()` (src/client/stellata.ts:2912-2914) currently disposes only a subset of GPU resources. The following are not released:

- `coreMaskMaterial` (separate from the three star materials)
- `particleMesh.geometry` and `particleMaterial`
- `clouds` (MolecularClouds layer)
- `galacticDisc`
- `galacticGrid`
- `hudOverlay` if it owns any GPU resources

In Three.js, every Material/Geometry/Texture/RenderTarget allocates GPU memory and must be `.dispose()`d explicitly. The current dispose() leaks all of the above on teardown. Today this is academic since dispose() runs only on page navigation, but it would matter for any future hot-reload / re-init path and for the in-flight tests around scene swapping.

Fix: Walk the dispose chain end-to-end and verify each layer/material is released. Easiest path: have each owned layer expose its own `dispose()` and call it from `Stellata.dispose()`.

Severity: CRITICAL (real leak, but currently low-impact).
close_reason: Fixed in PR #14 — dispose() now releases coreMaskMaterial, particleMesh+particleMaterial, clouds, galacticDisc, galacticGrid alongside the previously-disposed star geometry/materials and milkyway.

title: Thorough code review of production code for quality issues
description:
Conduct a comprehensive code review of all production code in src/ and scripts/ looking for:

- Inefficiencies (hot path waste, redundant work, suboptimal data structures)
- Bad style (inconsistent naming, unclear control flow, awkward abstractions)
- DRY violations (duplicated logic, repeated patterns that should be extracted)
- Dead code (unused exports, unreachable branches, stale flags)
- Premature abstractions or over-engineering
- Missing or misplaced comments (WHY comments needed; WHAT comments to remove)
- Unnecessary complexity (could be simpler without losing capability)
- Type-safety gaps or any-typed escape hatches
- Resource handling (event listener leaks, observer cleanup, GPU resource disposal)

Output: a triaged list of findings, each with file:line references and a recommended fix. Group by severity. File follow-up beads issues for non-trivial fixes; apply trivial cleanups directly.
notes:
Reviewed all 54 production source files (12,735 LOC) across src/ and scripts/. Triaged findings into 22 follow-up issues filed with `discovered-from:stellata-y84`.

**P1 (critical)** — 2 issues:
- stellata-c2l: GPU disposal audit (Stellata.dispose leaks)
- stellata-7is: 0x10 flag bit + inferBinaries pairing semantics

**P2 (high)** — 11 issues:
- Bugs: stellata-3s2, stellata-470, stellata-6oy, stellata-adr, stellata-mu9
- Perf: stellata-0a7 (shader dust prefilter), stellata-20w (pickStar), stellata-63f (Vector3 alloc), stellata-a0n (url-state hash), stellata-akk (SVG dirty-track), stellata-eya (typeahead arrow-nav)

**P3 (medium)** — 9 issues:
- DRY: stellata-snv, stellata-vjj, stellata-wk2, stellata-77m, stellata-v2j, stellata-075
- Cleanup: stellata-r48, stellata-t6l, stellata-nqb (rollup of small comment/WHY/dead-code findings)

**Trivial cleanups applied directly** (single commit):
- chart-labels.ts:240 — `let` → `const lastCentroidCamPos`
- chart-mode.ts:33 — `bindChartMode` return type `: void`
- molecular-clouds.ts:184 — `idx ?? null` simplify
- theme-toggle.ts — drop unused `getTheme()` + `currentMode` write-only state
- milkyway.ts:120 — collapse unused `GAL_TO_ICRS_M3` into one-line `ICRS_TO_GAL_M3` derivation

Typecheck clean, 244/244 tests pass.

**Overall impressions** from agents (synthesized):
- Hot-path discipline is generally strong (scratch quaternions, sortedDistFromSol prefilter for the core-mask gate, instanced star pipeline) but a recurring theme is per-frame Vector3 allocation across stellata.ts, the SVG overlays, and url-state. Adopting the `xxxInto(idx, out)` and dirty-tracked attribute write patterns from chart-labels.ts is the single biggest perf win available.
- Shader hygiene is good (calibration comments on every magic uniform); biggest single GPU win is moving the 48-tap `dustExtinctionAV` raymarch below the visibility prefilter.
- Type safety has one concrete hole (url-state FilterState patch) plus untyped CSV row + search-index entry in build-catalog.
- Binary format hygiene is solid but constants are duplicated across writer/verifier/reader; a shared layout map would prevent silent drift.
- DRY: clearest wins are modal dismissal (×3 modals), hud-overlay/poi-overlay arrow algorithm (~80 LOC duplicated), SearchBox vs constellation-typeahead (parallel implementations of the same UI), eight on*Handlers arrays in stellata.ts.

title: Rename version-guard job key to match workflow name
description: Branch protection's required-check rule `version-guard` never matched because the reported check name was `check` (the job key under `jobs:`), displayed as `version-guard / check` in the merge box. BP matches the job name, not the slash-display string. Renaming the job key to `version-guard` (mirroring `test.yml`'s self-named pattern) makes the reported check just `version-guard`, restoring the user's original BP rule.

title: 61 Cyg: disc-edge falloff blends unnaturally where two star discs overlap
description:
Visual artifact observed in 61 Cyg A/B (and likely all close binaries with similar-sized discs and tight on-screen separation, e.g. α Cen) when the two discs are close enough on screen that their soft super-Gaussian edges intersect.

# What you see

User-reported in 61 Cyg with two screenshots:
- View 1: the smaller (B, K7V, absmag ≈ 8.33) and larger (A, K5V, absmag ≈ 7.49) discs partially overlap. Where their edges meet, the boundary forms a sharp lens-shaped intersection that reads as a seam rather than a natural blend.
- View 2: the discs are nearly tangent. Where their fuzzy outer falloffs touch, the *junction* brightens unnaturally — the additive contribution of two falloffs in the same screen pixel exceeds either source individually, producing a visible bright cusp at the contact point.

# What's happening (working theory)

The disc pass uses the unified super-Gaussian profile (see docs/rendering.md). Two overlapping disc instances both write to the same screen pixel, and the blend mode for the disc pass is *additive* on the falloff edge — the depth-mask core is opaque, but the outer ring contributes additively, so two falloffs sum.

For widely-separated stars this is invisible (no shared pixels). For tight binaries imaged near minimum-distance the falloffs overlap and sum.

# Fix directions to consider (not for this ticket)

- Switch the disc-edge blend from additive to a max() / replace, so two overlapping falloffs don't sum.
- Per-system depth ordering: opaque disc of the nearer star occludes the falloff of the farther star.
- Re-tune the super-Gaussian outer falloff so the blend region is sharper (less overlap zone in screen pixels).

# Repro

Focus 61 Cyg A in navigate mode, zoom toward minimum distance until both discs are visible side-by-side. Compare to a single isolated star (e.g. Sol) at the same fill-fraction — the edge does not look this way.

title: Touch gestures: pinch-to-zoom + multi-touch behaviour on iPad / touch devices
description:
The mobile epic body already flags 'no pinch-to-zoom, two-finger roll is desktop-Safari-only by design' as a known gap. Even on iPad-sized viewports — which 47c.3 may decide is the only fully-supported mobile target — the current camera doesn't support pinch-to-zoom, so 'works on iPad' is currently aspirational.

WHAT'S THERE TODAY (src/client/stellata.ts + observe-controls.ts):
- TrackballControls (Three.js) drives the navigate camera. Its built-in touch handling is single-finger orbit + (we believe) two-finger pan. Pinch-to-zoom is NOT a TrackballControls feature.
- observe-controls.ts handles the OBSERVE camera (drag yaw + pitch, wheel FOV). No touch pinch.
- Two-finger roll exists ONLY on desktop Safari (gesturestart/change events) per docs/camera-modes.md.
- touch-action: none is set on #scene to prevent default browser gestures on the canvas.

WHAT NEEDS WORK:
1. Verify on iPad: do single-finger orbit + double-tap-zoom + the pan gesture actually work via TrackballControls' touch path, or are they broken too?
2. Pinch-to-zoom (navigate mode):
   - Map pinch span change to camera distance (matches scroll-wheel behaviour).
   - Implement via TouchEvent (iOS-friendly) since gesture* events are Safari-desktop-only.
3. Pinch-to-FOV (observe mode):
   - Same gesture, mapped to camera.fov instead of distance.
4. Decide what the two-finger drag does:
   - On desktop today: nothing in canvas (mouse drag = orbit, wheel = zoom).
   - On touch: Three.js TrackballControls treats two-finger drag as pan. We may want to keep that, or override to roll, or disable.
5. Single-finger drag in OBSERVE mode:
   - Currently observe-controls.ts uses pointerdown/move; should already work on touch since pointer events fire for touch. Verify.
6. Confirm the touch-action: none on #scene doesn't kill the gesture handlers we add.

CRITICAL FILES:
- src/client/stellata.ts — TrackballControls instance, existing onTouchStart/Move/End (currently used only for two-finger roll detection).
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — OBSERVE camera; pointer-event-based.
- node_modules/three/examples/jsm/controls/TrackballControls.js — to understand existing touch behaviour before extending.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing the desktop control scheme; replacing TrackballControls wholesale (the epic explicitly excludes that).

title: Decision: minimum viewport policy + mobile gating UX
description:
Decide how Stellata behaves on viewports too small for the desktop-tuned layout. The current settings panel + topbar overlap the canvas badly on phone-width devices, and full-bleed is impractical in browser iOS Safari (see 47c.2 notes). Several distinct UX paths are possible — pick one before sinking effort into per-control mobile redesigns.

OPTIONS to evaluate (these are the design space; deliverable is a chosen direction + threshold + copy):

A. Hard block below threshold
   - At <X width, replace the app with a simple gate: 'Stellata is built for tablet and desktop. Open this on a larger screen, or Add to Home Screen for an immersive full-screen experience on this device.'
   - Pros: zero effort tuning per phone breakpoint; one consistent fallback.
   - Cons: refuses users; may feel aggressive.

B. Soft warning modal (dismissable)
   - First-launch detect: small viewport → show modal explaining caveats + offering 'Add to Home Screen' instructions, dismissable to continue with degraded UX.
   - Pros: respects user agency.
   - Cons: more code, easy to dismiss-and-forget.

C. Banner/toast (always visible)
   - Persistent thin banner: 'Best on tablet/desktop · Add to Home Screen for full-screen'.
   - Pros: low-effort.
   - Cons: takes vertical space; nags.

D. Just polish best we can, no gating
   - Spend the effort making the panel collapsible, controls touch-friendly, modals reflow.
   - Pros: most users get a usable experience.
   - Cons: a lot of work, never quite as good as desktop, harder to QA.

E. Hybrid: D for tablet (≥768 width), A or B for phone (<480 or <600 width)

THRESHOLD QUESTION: where's the cutoff?
- iPad mini (portrait) is ~768 width × 1024 height — likely OK with desktop layout.
- iPhone Pro Max (portrait) is ~430 width × 932 height — settings panel covers ~65% of width.
- iPhone SE (portrait) is ~375 width — even worse.
- Landscape phones (e.g. 932 × 430) have ample width but very short height.

Recommend deciding on width-based gating in portrait; treat landscape phone as 'works but tight'.

ADD-TO-HOME-SCREEN COPY:
- If we recommend AtHS in the gating UI, draft the instructions inline. Apple's icon for the share sheet has changed across iOS versions; either show a generic step-by-step or a screenshot.

INPUTS to this decision (gather before deciding):
- Walk through panel + modals on real iPhone (covered by 47c.1).
- Render perf at iPhone resolution: how bad is it really? (Also 47c.1.)
- Telemetry: do we have any data on phone visits today? (Probably not; treat as unknown.)

DELIVERABLE: a written decision (which option + threshold + AtHS copy) attached to this issue, then file follow-up issues under 47c that *implement* that decision.

title: Search/typeahead: keep DOM, only toggle .active class on arrow-nav
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/search.ts:94 and src/client/constellation-typeahead.ts:69 both use the same anti-pattern: every keystroke AND every ArrowUp/ArrowDown nav rebuilds the entire results `<ul>` via `resultsEl.innerHTML = ''` followed by per-result `appendChild`. Arrow-nav only changes which row has the `active` class; tearing down and rebuilding the whole list is unnecessary and noticeable as jank with the typeahead's 88-entry constellation list.

Fix: For arrow-nav, just toggle the `active` class on the previously-hovered and newly-hovered `<li>` in place. Rebuild only when the query changes.

Severity: HIGH (visible UX latency on arrow-nav).

title: FilterState patch type-safety hole at url-state.ts:659
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/url-state.ts:659 — the `patch` object passed to `setFilter` is typed as `Record<string, number | boolean>`. This defeats `FilterState`'s field-name type checking: typos in keys like `'minDistSol'` or `'sizeMinOverridden'` would compile silently.

Fix: Declare `const patch: Partial<FilterState> = {};` directly. All keys assigned are already `keyof FilterState`, so the Partial form will catch typos at compile time.

Related (smaller): src/client/main.ts:74 casts `(window as unknown as { stellata: Stellata }).stellata = stellata;` — better to declare the global in src/client/globals.d.ts and assign without the cast.

Severity: HIGH (real type-safety hole, single-file fix).

title: SVG overlays: dirty-track attribute writes + scratch-vector projection helpers
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

Several SVG overlays unconditionally rewrite attributes / textContent every animate tick. The dirty-tracking pattern from chart-labels.ts (compare proposed value to lastWritten, skip identical) is missing across:

- **hud-overlay.ts:308-318** — `path.setAttribute('d', d)`, `bg.setAttribute('d', d)`, `label.setAttribute('x'/'y', ...)`, `label.textContent = ...` per frame for both Sol and GC arrows.
- **poi-overlay.ts:88-92, 73-86, 191-204, 328-352, 362** — text and class writes every frame for every POI.
- **disc-mask.ts:46-61** — focused star + companion `cx/cy/r` writes every frame.
- **distance-vector-overlay.ts** — same kind of per-frame attribute writes; plus `getComputedTextLength()` at line 139 forces SVG layout every frame even when the destination label is unchanged.
- **constellation-overlay.ts:50-72** — segment string `setAttribute('d', ...)` per frame for every constellation in chart mode.

Also: **projection helpers in poi-overlay.ts:391, hud-overlay.ts:400, distance-vector-overlay.ts:161-179** all `.clone()` Vector3 inputs per call. Per frame × per POI/arrow this is the biggest non-obvious allocation cluster outside stellata.ts itself.

Fix: Adopt `lastD`, `lastText`, `lastX`, `lastY`, `lastR` fields per element; gate writes on change. Replace `.clone()` projection helpers with module-scope scratch `Vector3`s (poi-overlay already has the pattern for `tmpStarLocal` / `tmpDir` etc). Cache `getComputedTextLength` keyed on text content.

Severity: HIGH (multiple files, but each small; together they're the steady-state per-frame DOM cost).

title: url-state: drop per-frame toFixed×9 string hash for change detection
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/url-state.ts:781-810 — `startUrlSync` `onFrame` callback constructs a 9-component string-formatted hash every frame via `toFixed(3) × 9` plus template literal. That is 10+ string allocations per frame just to detect change in the steady idle state.

Fix: Replace with component-wise epsilon comparison against scratch Float64s for cam/tgt/up. Schedule the URL update only on first non-equal check. No allocations on the no-change path.

Severity: HIGH (steady-state per-frame allocation, easy fix).

title: pickStar 313k-star linear scan: prefilter window + hoist constants
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/stellata.ts:2133-2178 — `pickStar` linearly scans all 313k stars on every hover (after 280ms throttle) and every click. For each star, it calls `renderedSizePx` (line 2237-2297) which re-reads ~10 uniforms and recomputes log/sqrt/pow per star.

Two improvements:

1. **Bound the scan** — the existing `sortedDistFromSol` prefilter used by the core-mask gate can window the scan: binary-search the slice `[f.minDistSol, f.maxDistSol]` and iterate only stars in that range.

2. **Hoist constants out of the loop** — `renderedSizePx` reads uniforms inside; in `pickStar`'s loop these are constant per call. Inline the disc-size formula with hoisted uniform reads, or pass an "ambient state" struct in.

Also flagged: `renderedSizePx` and `minDistForStar` (lines 2329-2350) duplicate the `physMinPx + logRatio*(physMaxPx-physMinPx)` math. The companion-separation block at lines 2306-2316 and 2342-2350 is also duplicated. Consider a private `_physSizeAtRef(idx)` helper.

Severity: HIGH (hover throttle masks the cost but click latency is user-visible).

title: stellata.ts hot-path Vector3 allocations: adopt xxxInto(idx, out) pattern
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

stellata.ts: several helpers return a fresh `THREE.Vector3` and are called from per-frame paths:

- `starLocalPosition(idx)` (line 2093-2096) — called by overlays per frame, by HUD, by `pickStar`'s 313k loop, by `updateGalacticLayers` (line 2722), by `updateWarp` (lines 2784, 2802)
- `cloudLocalPosition(idx)` — same problem in cloud-warp paths
- `currentFocusLocalPos()` (line 1689-1696) — uses `.clone()`

Each call allocates a new Vector3. In steady-state this is 5-10 GC-eligible allocations per frame.

Fix: Add `xxxInto(idx, out: THREE.Vector3): THREE.Vector3` variants that write into a caller-provided scratch vector. Migrate hot-path callers (`animate()`, `updateGalacticLayers`, `updateWarp`, `pickStar`'s loop, HUD update). Either remove the allocating shims or keep them only for cold paths (initial focus selection, click handlers).

Severity: HIGH (steady-state GC pressure on the per-frame path).

title: Star vertex shader: move dustExtinctionAV below visibility prefilter
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:225 — `dustExtinctionAV` runs a 48-tap 3D-texture raymarch through the dust field for **every star instance every frame**, including stars that fail the visibility prefilter (`visible`/`magOk`/`distOk`/`spectOk`) further down the shader (line 244+). With 313k stars, this is the dominant vertex-shader cost.

Fix: Move the visibility prefilter above the dust raymarch. Compute the unextincted apparent magnitude first, fail-fast on stars that are out of band, then refine with extinction only for the survivors.

Caveat: stars near the magnitude threshold could pop in/out as extinction is applied/skipped. May need a small magnitude buffer (e.g. allow extra 0.5 mag headroom in the prefilter so dust-darkened stars within tolerance still get the raymarch).

Severity: HIGH (single biggest per-frame GPU win in the shader chunk).

title: unit-toggle: hidden module-level singleton + redundant applyUnit shim
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/unit-toggle.ts has two issues:

1. **Hidden module-level singleton**: line 3 caches `buttons` at module scope. `bindUnitToggle` populates it. A second call to `bindUnitToggle` would silently overwrite the cache and abandon the previously-attached listeners — causing them to fire on the wrong button references. Currently called only once, so the bug is latent, but the API doesn't enforce single-call.

Fix: Capture `buttons` in a closure inside `bindUnitToggle`; pass it explicitly to `syncButtons`.

2. **`applyUnit` shim** (line 15): one-line wrapper around `setUnit`. It IS used by `url-state.ts:655`, so it cannot be deleted, but it's a redundant indirection.

Fix: Inline `applyUnit` callers to call `setUnit` directly, then delete `applyUnit`.

Severity: HIGH (hidden singleton is a real bug-in-waiting; wrapper is style).

title: observe-controls: missing pointercancel handler causes stuck drag state
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/observe-controls.ts handles `pointerdown`/`pointermove`/`pointerup` for look-around drag. It is missing a `pointercancel` handler. If the OS preempts the pointer mid-drag (touch interrupted by a phone call, system gesture stealing the pointer, browser cancelling the touch), `dragging` stays `true` and the next `pointermove` continues a phantom drag — sometimes with momentum applied.

Fix: Add a `pointercancel` listener that mirrors `onPointerUp` (probably without the momentum-promotion path — a cancelled drag shouldn't feel like the user released).

Severity: HIGH (real UX bug, hits mobile users specifically).

title: MolecularClouds.setIsobar: uniform reference mutation + missing blending restore
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

molecular-clouds.ts:117-129 (`setIsobar`):

1. `mat.uniforms.uMaxAppMag = magnitudeUniform` — replaces the `{value:...}` reference every call. If a caller passes a different shared uniform on a subsequent invocation, prior bindings are silently abandoned. Three.js convention is to mutate `.value`, not the wrapper object.
2. When `on === false`, `mat.blending` isn't restored. Clouds remain in their isobar blending mode after the toggle off, until `setMonochrome` is called again (which today only happens on theme change).

Note: the molecular-cloud overlay is shelved for v1 (CLAUDE.md), so this is latent — it will bite when the layer is re-enabled.

Fix:
- Set the uniform reference once at construction (or first call) and afterwards only mutate `.value`.
- Restore `mat.blending` to the appropriate non-isobar mode when `on === false`.

Severity: HIGH (latent bug — would manifest on chart-mode toggle once clouds are re-enabled).

title: dust-loader: validate buffer size + handle WebGL context loss
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

**dust-loader.ts:73-81** — When parsing a `particles.bin` file, the count check at line 73 only warns; a length mismatch causes either a Float32Array constructor throw ("byte length should be a multiple of 4") or an out-of-bounds view. The header `count` is taken from the `manifest.json` but the actual file size isn't validated.

Fix: After parsing the header `count`, assert `ab.byteLength === 16 + count * 16` and bail out (return null) on mismatch, instead of using `meta.count` as the source of truth for the allocation size.

**dust-loader.ts:226-258** — `uploadChunk` reaches into `renderer.properties.get(tex).__webglTexture` (undocumented Three.js private API). There is no fallback on context loss; the code logs and silently drops the chunk, leaving permanent holes in the dust grid.

Fix: Add `webglcontextlost` / `webglcontextrestored` listeners to the canvas; on restore, re-upload all already-loaded chunks from cached source data. Or at minimum surface the failure prominently.

Severity: HIGH (correctness on edge cases — bad files, context loss).

title: observe→navigate exit: camera roll snap proportional to look-around in observe
description:
When exiting OBSERVE → navigate after rotating the camera (yaw/pitch via drag) inside observe, there is a visible camera jump at the end of the exit lerp. The magnitude of the jump scales with how much the camera was rotated in observe mode.

# Likely root cause (from a quick code read — confirm during fix)

`stellata.ts:startObserveExit` (~line 990) translates the camera backward along its current forward vector by `minDist` — preserving the eye→target axis (target = star, at fromPos). `finishObserveTransition` then runs `this.controls.target.copy(state.fromPos); this.controls.update();`.

The translation is correct (camera and target are collinear with the rotated look direction, so lookAt's rotation about the eye→target axis would be a no-op). What jumps is **roll**: TrackballControls.update() runs lookAt(target), which uses `camera.up` to resolve the roll axis. The observe look-around controller pitches the camera quaternion without updating `camera.up`, so when lookAt re-aligns to `camera.up = (0,1,0)` it removes the accumulated pitch's tilt as a roll snap.

Reproduces on the deployed build — pre-exists the a7d.1 log-depth-buffer change.

# Suspected fix surfaces

- `src/client/observe-controls.ts` — keep `camera.up` consistent with the look quaternion as the user pitches; or
- `src/client/stellata.ts:startObserveExit` / `finishObserveTransition` — set `camera.up` from the current quaternion before re-enabling controls so lookAt is roll-stable.

# Verification

- Focus a star, enter observe, drag yaw + pitch noticeably, exit observe via the mode toggle. The end-of-lerp frame should match the previous frame's roll exactly — no snap.
- Repeat with the search-X clear path (`clearFocusOnExit=true`) and the no-animate hard-switch path (no focus).
- `npm test` + `npm run typecheck` clean.

title: Chart-mode: nebulae outlines per type symbology
description:
**Goal:** render catalogued nebulae from `stellata-9hp` as chart-mode outlines using the per-type symbology defined in `stellata-m40.1`.

**Implementation:**
- Extend `chart-mode.ts` to register a nebulae-overlay path active in chart mode
- Per-nebula glyph in `chart-labels.ts` (or a new `chart-nebulae.ts` if the existing label engine isn't a clean fit)
- Type-specific symbology from `m40.1`: PNe symbol, H II outline, RNe outline, SNR outline
- Picking + label coordinates with existing chart-mode label engine

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/chart-mode.ts` — orchestrator hook for the nebulae layer
- `src/client/chart-labels.ts` — glyph integration (or new module)
- Possibly `src/client/shaders/nebula.frag.glsl` (from `stellata-9hp.2`) gets a chart-mode branch like the cloud shader's `uChartIsobar` path

**Visual gate:** a region rich in catalogued nebulae (e.g. Orion's belt + sword) reads as a star atlas — outlines indicate nebula presence and approximate extent without dominating the page.

**Depends on:** `stellata-m40.1` (symbology design) + `stellata-9hp` epic shipping (nebulae data + base rendering).

title: Design: chart-mode visual language for catalogued structures (Sky Atlas 2000.0 reference)
description:
**Goal:** produce the design reference that drives `m40`'s child rendering tasks. Output is a written section added to `docs/chart-mode.md` covering per-structure-type chart symbology.

**Reference:** Sky Atlas 2000.0 conventions (user has a scanned reference page available on request). Other classical atlases (Norton's, Uranometria) are secondary references if useful.

**Per-type symbology to define:**
- **Nebulae** — by type (PNe, RNe, H II, SNR), each has traditional chart symbol conventions:
  - PNe: small green-tinged circle with cross or shell-style outline
  - H II / diffuse: dashed or solid outline approximating the angular extent
  - RNe: outline biased toward illuminator, often dashed
  - SNR: filamentary or shell outline
- **Solar-system planets** — labelled symbol (♀ ♂ ♃ ♄ etc. or filled disc with name) at current angular position, sized appropriately for the planet vs. the chart star labels

**Constraints:**
- Chart mode is monochrome (existing convention) — colour can't differentiate types; line style and shape carry the encoding
- Outlines should read at the same visual weight as constellation lines, neither dominating nor disappearing
- Coordinate with the per-frame label engine in `chart-labels.ts` so symbols don't fight existing star/constellation labels for space

**Output:** new subsection in `docs/chart-mode.md` with reference symbology table + opacity/weight values. Drives `m40.2` and `m40.3` implementation.

**No code in this phase.**

title: Chart-mode rendering of catalogued structures (nebulae, planets, etc.)
description:
**Goal:** as new structures land in the model (nebulae, solar-system planets, etc.), they need a chart-mode representation when they're visually relevant in the real sky. Traditional star atlases (Sky Atlas 2000.0 is the canonical reference) render bright/catalogued objects as light outlines so observers can use the chart against the actual sky.

**Per-structure visibility decision** (made up-front so each structure's chart-mode treatment is clear):

| Structure | Visually relevant? | Chart-mode? |
|---|---|---|
| Stars (existing) | yes | ✓ shipped |
| Constellation lines (existing) | reference | ✓ shipped |
| Galactic disc (existing) | yes (band of MW) | ✓ shipped (isobar) |
| **Nebulae (PNe, RNe, H II, SNR)** — `stellata-9hp` | **YES** | **add** |
| **Solar-system planets** — `stellata-3re.4` | **YES** (Mercury through Saturn naked-eye) | **add** |
| Molecular clouds — `stellata-c7u` | no (extinction is the signal, dark patches at most) | skip |
| Local Bubble — `stellata-dk3` | no (3D cavity, invisible) | skip |
| Radcliffe Wave — `stellata-n2x` | no (3D structure) | skip |
| Heliopause / scale rings — `stellata-3re.5` | no | skip |
| Exoplanets — `stellata-bk5` | no | skip |
| Deep-space probes — `stellata-ywn` | no | skip |

**Children:**
- `.1` Design language — chart-mode visual conventions per structure type (line weights, fills, opacity, label conventions). User has a scanned Sky Atlas 2000.0 reference page available on request. Output: written reference in `docs/chart-mode.md` covering the per-type symbology rules.
- `.2` Nebulae chart-mode rendering (depends on `stellata-9hp` shipping + `.1` design)
- `.3` Solar-system planet chart-mode rendering (depends on `stellata-3re.4` shipping + `.1` design)

**Future:** any new structure added to the model needs a chart-mode decision — visible? render. invisible? skip. Add a child here when the call is 'render'.

**Out of scope:**
- Any change to existing chart-mode shipped behaviour (stars, constellation lines, galactic disc isobar)
- The constellation-name label-size tweak (filed separately as its own task)

title: Search index integration + RNe illuminator cross-match to ATHYG
description:
**Goal:** named nebulae appear in the search bar like stars and clouds; reflection nebulae's illuminating stars are resolved to ATHYG indices for colour-biasing in `stellata-9hp.3`.

**Search index:**
- Extend `search-index.json` to include nebula entries with `kind: 'nebula'` discriminator (existing pattern: stars use `kind: 'star'`, clouds use `kind: 'cloud'`)
- Common-name + type-prefix entries (e.g. 'Orion Nebula', 'Ring Nebula', 'M42')
- Focus search box dispatches to `flyToNebula` (analogous to `flyToCloud` from the cloud overlay)
- `stellata.ts` gains `flyToNebula(nebulaIdx)` mirroring `flyToCloud`

**RNe illuminator cross-match:**
- Magakian RNe entries name an illuminating star (HD/HIP). Resolve at build time via the same hipToIndex / hd → index maps used by `build-catalog.ts`
- Emit `illuminator_idx: number | null` per RNe entry in `nebulae.json`
- Renderer (in `stellata-9hp.3`) uses the illuminator's colour to bias the bluish reflection tint

**Depends on:** `stellata-9hp.1` for the nebulae data file.

title: Type-specific visual treatments (H II, PNe, RNe, SNR)
description:
**Goal:** distinguishable visuals per nebula type so the type encoding reads at a glance. All visuals procedural — **no photographs**, matching the rest of the app's aesthetic.

**Per-type treatment** (full notes in parent `stellata-9hp`):
- **H II regions** — warm reddish-pink, soft sphere with density falloff
- **Planetary nebulae** — greenish-teal, often shell-like; small central point + thin shell
- **Reflection nebulae** — bluish, irregular blob biased toward the illuminating star's colour (look up via ATHYG cross-ref from `stellata-9hp.4`)
- **Supernova remnants** — filamentary; pink → blue gradient by age

**Approach:** type-specific density profile + colour mapping passed as uniforms to the raymarch shader from `stellata-9hp.2`. Procedural shape variation per type is essential — without it, every nebula renders as a blob and the type encoding is lost.

**Depends on:** `stellata-9hp.2` (raymarch shader) and `stellata-9hp.4` (RNe illuminator cross-match for the bluish-toward-illuminator bias).

title: Per-nebula raymarching shader (small AABB volumetrics)
description:
**Goal:** the volumetric rendering primitive for nebulae. Each nebula occupies a small AABB (typically 1–50 pc), so per-nebula raymarching with 16–32 samples is dense in voxel-space and produces smooth volumetrics without the aliasing that killed the all-sky fog approach.

**Reference:** recover the fog shader pattern from git history at commit `5ad5444` — clip-space fullscreen triangle, ray-AABB slab intersection, fixed-step march with log-encoded density decode. See `memory/feedback_raymarch_for_nebulae.md` for context on why the all-sky version failed and per-nebula AABBs work.

**Implementation:**
- New shader pair `src/client/shaders/nebula.{vert,frag}.glsl`
- Per-nebula uniform set: AABB centre + extents, density profile parameters, type-coded colour (set by `stellata-9hp.3`)
- Render-order placement coordinated with stars / clouds / Milky Way

**Out of scope:** type-specific procedural shape variation lives in `stellata-9hp.3`. This issue delivers the raymarch primitive with a single neutral density profile.

**Depends on:** `stellata-9hp.1` (data) for AABBs to render against.

title: Unified nebulae data ingest pipeline (PNe + RNe + H II + SNR)
description:
**Goal:** produce `public/nebulae.json` from the four catalogues, filtered to within ~3 kpc to match the dust volume, with consistent per-nebula schema.

**Sources** (full details in parent `stellata-9hp`):
- Strasbourg-ESO PNe (Acker et al., VizieR V/84) — ~1,500 PNe with multiple distance estimates
- Magakian RNe (J/A+A/399/141) — 913 reflection nebulae, illuminator cross-match needed
- Sharpless H II (VII/20) — 313 entries, pair with Avedisova (V/115) for distances
- SIMBAD TAP query as the unified fallback for object types PN/RNe/HII/SNR

**Per-nebula schema:** ID, common name, type (PN|RNe|HII|SNR), RA/Dec, distance, → xyz, angular size → physical radius, illuminating star ref (RNe only).

**Output:** new `scripts/build-nebulae.py` (mirroring `build-clouds.py` pattern) emitting `public/nebulae.json`. Add to `.gitignore`. Wire into `dev` and `build` scripts.

**Watch out for:**
- Drop entries with no parsed distance
- Filter to ATHYG-relevant volume; >5 kpc clutters search without rendering usefully
- RNe illuminators: parse HD/HIP from source, leave the cross-match to `stellata-9hp.4`

title: Evaluate perf + first-load tradeoffs: reduced_m12 (~2M stars) vs reduced_m11 (~800k)
description:
**Goal:** decide which ATHYG reduced subset the catalog upgrade in `stellata-cns` should target. The parent issue currently specifies `reduced_m12` (~2M stars, ~80 MB binary, ~20 MB gzipped). `reduced_m11` (~800k stars) is the smaller alternative.

**Tradeoffs to measure:**
- **First-load size**: m12 ~20 MB gzipped vs m11 estimated ~8 MB. Significant for slow connections and mobile.
- **Render perf**: 2M instanced quads at 60 fps on integrated GPUs / mobile chips vs ~800k. Three.js instance-attribute memory grows linearly. Two-pass core/glow split helps overdraw, but instance count is the dominant cost.
- **Scientific completeness**: m12 reaches further into the kpc range; m11 truncates at brighter apparent mag and leaves more gaps at intermediate distances (50 pc – kpc).
- **Build pipeline cost**: m12 CSV is ~400 MB (LFS); m11 is smaller. Build time + CI implications.

**Method:**
- Pull both ATHYG subsets and run `scripts/build-catalog.ts` against each
- Measure: binary size, gzipped wire size, instance-attribute memory, FPS on a representative low-end target (integrated GPU, ideally a mid-tier phone)
- Compare scientific feel: are intermediate-distance gaps in m11 visually obvious during travel?

**Output:** a recommendation in this issue's notes for which subset `stellata-cns` should target. Update `cns` accordingly when decided.

**Watch out for:**
- m11 ⊂ m12 ⊂ `reduced_m13.5` etc. — all are subsets of full ATHYG; differences are purely the apparent-mag cutoff.
- Mobile perf may be the deciding factor — if m12 doesn't run on phones, the answer is m11 regardless of completeness.
notes: 2026-05-02 — note: this decision sets the **model's magnitude floor**, not just the star catalog cutoff. Whichever cutoff (m11 or m12) wins, it becomes the dimnest threshold for all object categories — variable stars, nebulae, projected distant DSOs (`stellata-38m` and the new projected-DSOs task), and any future object types. Factor this into the decision: m11 (~mag 11 floor) gives a cleaner, brighter sky; m12 (~mag 12 floor) gives a deeper sky but more visual density and more catalog work for every object type that has to respect the floor.

title: A.1 Research + design doc: physics model for molecular cloud rendering
description:
**Goal:** produce the implementation design before any rendering code is written. Output is a written model in `docs/molecular-clouds.md` (replacing the current shelved-state blurb) that drives phases A.2–A.7.

**Research scope:**
- **Extinction physics**: τ = ∫κ·ρ dl. Map Zucker bbox + assumed density to optical depth at V band. Calibrate κ × ρ_total so a typical dense cloud (e.g. Taurus core) produces realistic Av (~5–25 mag). Sanity-check against published Av values for known clouds.
- **Selective extinction (reddening)**: confirm RV ≈ 3.1 for diffuse ISM; note RV ≈ 5.5 for dense cloud cores. Cardelli-Clayton-Mathis (1989) parameterisation. Decide which RV to use (single value, or per-cloud-type, or per-cloud).
- **Density structure**: log-normal PDF (Vazquez-Semadeni 1994; Padoan, Nordlund & Jones 1997) + power-law tail at high densities (Federrath & Klessen 2013). Mach-number dependence. Filamentary substructure (Hi-GAL, Herschel filament catalogues).
- **Cloud taxonomy**: dark cloud vs GMC vs SF region. Which Zucker entries fall in each class? Use known classifications (Taurus dark-cloud, Orion A active SF, etc.) where available; default behaviour for unclassified.
- **Embedded young stars + cavities**: Strömgren-sphere radius for an O/B star in a cold MC, scaling with stellar luminosity and ambient density. Stellar-wind bubbles. How sharp is the cavity edge?
- **HII regions / reflection nebulosity**: when does an embedded star produce visible emission/scattering? Threshold spectral type / luminosity.
- **Inside-cloud experience**: what does a real observer at the centre of Taurus see? Reddened/dimmed background, very dark ambient — confirm this is the correct mental model.

**Design output (written into `docs/molecular-clouds.md`):**
- The per-cloud parameter set we'll bake at build time (centroid, semi-axes, peak density, density-profile-shape, embedded-star list, cavity radii).
- The shader-side model for the substructure noise (octaves, frequency, amplitude).
- The reddening model (B vs R extinction ratio).
- The integration strategy with the Edenhofer voxel field (replace, blend, or augment).
- Decision on whether the shelved `cloud.frag.glsl` warm-glow becomes A.6 or is replaced.
- Concrete equations, constants, and source citations.

**No code in this phase** — but should produce enough specificity that A.2–A.7 implementers can work without ambiguity.

title: Physical molecular cloud rendering — extinction, reddening, density, embedded-star cavities
description:
**Goal:** render the ~96 Zucker MCs with physically-grounded extinction, reddening, density structure, and cloud-type variation — the experience of actually flying through these clouds, to the best of current scientific knowledge. Plus a *very* subtle stylised "presence" so an empty-sky background still hints at cloud shape.

**Current state:** `cloud.frag.glsl` is decorative — `pow(|n·v|, 1.5)` × warm colour, additive blend. No extinction, no reddening, no real density. The layer is currently shelved (`FilterState.showMolecularClouds = false`). Re-enables once this epic lands.

**Decisions captured (from scoping discussion):**

1. **Realism-inspired.** Physically-correct extinction + reddening of background light is the primary visual. A *very subtle* stylised hint of cloud shape rides on top so users can perceive clouds against starless backgrounds. Stylisation must read as decorative, never compete with the physical signal.

2. **Procedural substructure** acceptable for v1. We won't search for higher-resolution per-cloud data products as part of this epic. (Separate future issue is filed for that as a follow-up.)

3. **Inside-the-cloud experience** should follow physics: stars whose line of sight traverses the cloud dim and redden, peripheral stars are unaffected, ambient gets very dark. Deep-research piece: surface ideas like *young massive stars carving low-density cavities via stellar winds and ionising radiation* — i.e., cloud density isn't a static envelope but is sculpted by embedded stars (Strömgren spheres, HII regions). Investigate whether modelling embedded O/B stars as low-density cavity centres with a radial density drop-off produces a believable inside-cloud experience.

4. **Cloud-type / age variation.** Model differently based on whether the cloud hosts young stars. Spectral type → age proxy gives a rough cloud-region age. HII regions, reflection nebulosity, and cavity-carving should be type-dependent (e.g., a quiescent dark cloud renders differently from an Orion-Nebula-class active SF region).

5. **Architecture: Zucker replaces Edenhofer in overlap regions.** In practice this likely means modifying the dust voxel volume at build time (`scripts/build-dust.py` + `manifest.json`) so the per-star extinction raymarch in `star.vert.glsl` becomes the single source of truth for absorption — Zucker supplies the higher-fidelity local density structure. Avoids double-counting.

**Phases (filed as child issues):**

- **A.1** Deep research + design doc — produce the physics model we'll implement and the equations/constants/references.
- **A.2** Build pipeline — bake Zucker density into the Edenhofer voxel grid at build time.
- **A.3** Wavelength-selective extinction in `star.vert.glsl` (B vs R, RV ≈ 3.1) — current path appears single-channel; confirm and add reddening.
- **A.4** Procedural density substructure (3D fBm/Worley noise shaped by the ellipsoid envelope, modulating density inside the bbox).
- **A.5** Embedded young-star cavities + cloud-type variations (cross-match O/B + YSO catalogues with cloud bboxes; reduced density near hot hosts; optional HII emission for the hottest).
- **A.6** Subtle stylised "presence" pass — re-frame or supersede the shelved warm-glow renderer; very subtle by design.
- **A.7** Inside-the-cloud immersion + ambient response tuning — confirm flying into Taurus reads as physics.

**Scope notes:**
- The currently shelved `cloud.frag.glsl` may be re-purposed as A.6's stylised presence or replaced — design-doc decision in A.1.
- Re-enables `FilterState.showMolecularClouds` (and possibly the URL flag) once the epic lands.
- No hard dependencies on other open issues.

**References to seed research:**
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_cloud
- Cardelli, Clayton, Mathis (1989) — selective-extinction law, RV ≈ 3.1
- Edenhofer et al. (2024) — current dust voxel field that this epic augments
- Zucker 2020 / 2021 — the cloud catalogue already in use (`data/molecular-clouds/`)
- Standard MC astrophysics: log-normal density PDF + power-law tail, Strömgren-sphere geometry, GMC vs dark-cloud taxonomy

title: Default first-load view: park near Sol with planets, Orion, and HUD on
description:
Replace the current "no URL state → drop the user into a generic starfield at `(0, 0, 30)`" default load behaviour with a deliberately framed first-impression view.

# Why

Stellata's first three seconds need to do emotional work. Today when someone loads the bare URL — no `cam=`, no `to=`, no view state — they see a vast unlabelled starfield with no anchor: no obvious "you are here," no implicit prompt to explore. Pretty, but inert.

A first-load that parks the camera near Sol with the Sun's disc clearly visible — at a distance that also frames the inner-solar-system planet orbits and a recognisable constellation in the background (Orion is the obvious pick — everyone in the northern hemisphere recognises the belt; equatorial latitude makes it visible from most of Earth) — gives the visitor an immediate story:

> *That's our Sun. Those are our planets. Orion is over there. Now look around.*

That single framing converts "what is this?" into "where do I want to go?" — and the rest of the app's exploration affordances (warp, focus, observe mode) follow naturally.

# What changes

When the URL has no view state on initial load:

1. **Camera focus = Sol** with disc clearly resolved.
2. **Camera distance** in the regime where the inner-solar-system planet orbits (Mercury through Mars at minimum, Jupiter ideally) are simultaneously visible. Probably tens of AU.
3. **Camera direction** chosen so Orion sits prominently in the background — likely centred, but a slightly off-centre framing might feel less staged. Defer the exact aim choice to implementation.
4. **HUD on** (`hud-ring` + coord sphere) so the first-time visitor immediately gets a sense of the celestial reference frame.
5. **Constellation overlay on** so Orion's stick figure is visible without the user having to discover the toggle.
6. **URL stays empty** until the user interacts. The default doesn't write itself to the URL — the share-link-equals-current-pose contract still holds; first interaction is the first URL write.

When the URL *does* carry view state, behaviour is unchanged.

# Why this is blocked by Phase 12a (and transitively by the rewrite)

- Sol must be a focusable target with a sensible close-approach floor. Today `controls.minDistance = 0.005 pc` (≈1031 AU) means we cannot park anywhere near tens-of-AU range from Sol. **Phase 12a (`stellata-3re`)** introduces the Sol-focus minDistance relaxation that makes this regime reachable.
- The Sun's disc must render at correct angular size at close range. **Phase 11 / the physical-scale rewrite (`stellata-a7d`)** — which Phase 12a already blocks on — replaces today's log-mapped pixel formula with `θ = 2·atan(R/d)`. Without it, the Sun's on-screen disc at the chosen distance won't reflect physical reality.
- Planet orbits to draw rings around come from **Phase 12a**.

This issue is the user-visible payoff of the physics + solar-system stack: the moment those two land, this becomes the natural default.

# Implementation sketch

- A new module (or a small block in `stellata.ts` bootstrap) decides the initial camera state when `url-state.ts` reports no view fragment present.
- Fixed values, hard-coded for the default — not a slider or preset:
  - Focus: Sol (catalog index TBD; Phase 12a will have established how Sol is referenced).
  - Distance: a single tuned constant (e.g. `~30 AU = ~1.45e-4 pc`) chosen so:
    - Sun's disc reads as a clear orb (R_Sol ≈ 4.65e-8 pc → θ ≈ 6.4e-4 rad ≈ 2.2 arcmin → at 50° FOV in a 1080-tall viewport that's ~24 px diameter — comfortably resolved).
    - Mars's orbit ring (1.52 AU) fits well inside the framing.
    - Jupiter's orbit ring (5.2 AU) likely also visible toward the edge.
  - Aim: a unit vector toward Orion's centroid (RA ≈ 5h 30m, Dec ≈ +0°). Compute once at startup or hard-code as an ICRS vector constant.
- HUD: ensure the existing HUD-on default isn't overridden by the new bootstrap path.
- Constellation overlay: same — turn on for Orion specifically, or all constellations (probably all, with Orion happening to be the focus of the framing).

# Open questions / deferred to implementation

- Whether to nudge the camera into a slow drift on first load (an autonomous "look, the world is alive" gesture) or leave it static. Lean static — drift is a separate Wander-mode-adjacent feature (`stellata-8r1`), and a static frame respects the user's first input as the start of *their* experience.
- Exact distance + aim — needs a screenshot pass once Phase 12a lands so we can pick numbers that feel right rather than just compute correctly.
- Whether the chart-mode default should differ. Probably no — chart mode is opt-in and inherits the same view.
- Mobile framing — viewport aspect changes the Orion centring story. Phase 47c (mobile UX audit) may need to feed back into this.

# Acceptance

1. Loading `stellata.app/` with no query string parks the camera at the framed Sol view.
2. Sol's disc is clearly visible (resolved, not a point glow).
3. At least the inner planet orbits (Mercury → Mars) are visible as orbit rings.
4. Orion's belt is visible and recognisable in the background, with the constellation stick-figure overlay drawn.
5. The HUD ring + coord sphere are on.
6. The URL bar shows no view-state parameters until the user interacts (drags, clicks a star, opens a modal that writes state, etc.).
7. Loading with view-state in the URL bypasses the framed default entirely — existing behaviour unchanged.

# Related work

- Blocked by Phase 12a (`stellata-3re`) — provides Sol-as-focus + planet orbits.
- Transitively depends on the physical-scale rewrite (`stellata-a7d`) via 12a — provides Sol's disc at correct angular size.
- Mobile layout caveat — `stellata-47c` may want to revisit framing on small viewports.
- Wander mode (`stellata-8r1`) is a separate idea; this issue deliberately stays static on first load.

title: Replace disc formula, orbit floor, and parking distance with true angular geometry
description:
Replace the log-mapped disc formula with the physical-angular-diameter formula `θ = 2·atan(R · radiusFactor / d)`, and drive the manual-zoom orbit floor + warp parking distance from the same per-star physical radius.

Depends on Phase 0 (log depth buffer) — without it, Sol-class stars will z-fight on close approach.

# Geometry

```
θ_rad      = 2 · atan(R · radiusFactor / d)
physSizePx = θ_rad · viewport.y / fov_y_rad
```

Slots in at `star.vert.glsl:294` (current `physSize = sizeAtRef * (uRefDistPc / dPc) * radiusFactor`). Same swap also needed inside the variability headroom precompute (`star.vert.glsl:203-206`).

`pxSize = max(appSize, physSize)` continues to give the natural blob↔disc crossover. `vPhysRatio = clamp(physSize / pxSize)` continues to drive the disc-pass / glow-pass split.

# Manual zoom floor (90% of fill-the-viewport)

Replaces `minOrbitDistForStar()` (`stellata.ts:2306-2316`):

```
ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.9
fov_y = camera.fov · π/180
fov_x = 2 · atan(tan(fov_y/2) · w/h)
fov_minor = min(fov_x, fov_y)
d_min(idx) = R_idx / tan(ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION · fov_minor / 2)
```

Preserve the binary half-angle bump (`Math.max(d_min, sep × BINARY_MIN_DIST_FACTOR)`).

Recompute on:
- Focus change (already wired at `stellata.ts:1045` and `:1876`).
- FOV change — wire into `setCameraFov()` (`stellata.ts:1473`).
- Window resize.

# Warp / observe-exit parking distance

Replaces `minDistForStar()` (`stellata.ts:2329-2350`):

```
TARGET_PARK_FRACTION = 0.10
d_park(idx) = R_idx / tan(TARGET_PARK_FRACTION · fov_minor / 2)
```

Floor at `2 × d_min(idx)` so the orbit floor never exceeds the parking distance. Preserve binary half-angle bump.

# CPU mirror

Rewrite `renderedSizePx()` (`stellata.ts:2237-2297`) to mirror the new geometry exactly. Variability `radiusFactor` continues to multiply `R` before the `atan`. `appSize` (perceptual blob) path stays unchanged.

# Uniforms — remove

| Removed                       | Reason                                                        |
|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| `uPhysMinPx` / `uPhysMaxPx`   | No more pixel-range mapping.                                  |
| `uLogRMin` / `uLogRMax`       | No more log-span normalisation.                               |
| `uRefDistPc`                  | No artificial reference distance.                             |
| `DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC = 0.005` | Replaced by per-star `d_min(R)`. Keep a small global default (e.g. `1e-9` pc) for the unfocused-around-origin orbit floor. |
| `TARGET_APPROACH_DISC_PX = 10`| Replaced by `TARGET_PARK_FRACTION = 0.10`.                    |

# Uniforms — add

- `uFovYRad` (float) — fed from `camera.fov · π/180`. Updated in the constructor and in `setCameraFov()`.
- (Alternative: derive from `uViewport` + a `uPxPerRad` scalar — `uFovYRad` is simpler.)

# Files

- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — replace physSize formula at line ~294 and ~203-206; drop dead uniform declarations; add `uFovYRad`.
- `src/client/stellata.ts`:
  - Line 468 — Phase 0 already touched this.
  - Lines 483-488 — Phase 0 already touched this.
  - Line 501 — `controls.minDistance` initial value uses the new global default.
  - Lines 593-616 (shared uniforms) — drop the dead uniforms; add `uFovYRad`.
  - Lines 2227-2229 — delete `computePhysMaxPx()`.
  - Lines 2237-2297 — rewrite `renderedSizePx()`.
  - Lines 2306-2316 — rewrite `minOrbitDistForStar()`.
  - Lines 2329-2350 — rewrite `minDistForStar()`.
  - Constants block (lines 171-190): remove `DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC`, `TARGET_APPROACH_DISC_PX`; add `TARGET_PARK_FRACTION`, `ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION`, `GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC` (e.g. `1e-9`).
  - Line 246 export — `DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC` is exported; `grep -rn DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC src/` and migrate or drop.
  - `setCameraFov()` (line 1473) — push the new `fov_y_rad` into `uFovYRad`.
- `src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:100` — switch destination offset from `getFilter().sizeMax` to `stellata.renderedSizePx(destIdx)` so the chevron lands on the disc edge regardless of star size.
- Docs (commit alongside): `docs/rendering.md`, `docs/camera-modes.md`.

# Verification (manual smoke + tests)

1. **Background stars unchanged.** Default view, naked-eye preset. Sirius / Betelgeuse / Vega pixel-identical to current `main`.
2. **Resolved-disc ratio matches physics.** Warp to Betelgeuse and Sirius A in turn at the same FOV. On-screen disc ratio at equal `d/R` matches `R_Betel / R_Sirius`. (Old behaviour: identical 10 px discs.)
3. **Close approach to Sol-class star.** Focus a Sol-like star, zoom in fully. Disc grows until ~90% of viewport minor axis, then stops. No clipping, no z-fighting.
4. **Close approach to a white dwarf** (40 Eridani B, Sirius B). Same — fills ~90% of viewport at d_min.

title: Enable log depth buffer + add <logdepthbuf> chunks to all shaders
description:
Prerequisite for the Phase 1 disc-formula rewrite. With per-star physical sizing, `d_min` for Sol-class stars drops to ~5×10⁻⁸ pc. Standard z-buffer precision collapses long before that. Without log depth buffer, Phase 1 will z-fight on close approach.

# Scope

- `stellata.ts:468` — set `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` on the `WebGLRenderer` constructor.
- `stellata.ts:483-488` — drop `camera.near` to `1e-10` (log depth handles the dynamic range).
- Add the standard log-depth chunks to every custom GLSL3 ShaderMaterial:
  - `src/client/shaders/star.{vert,frag}.glsl`
  - `src/client/shaders/cloud.{vert,frag}.glsl`
  - `src/client/shaders/dust-particle.{vert,frag}.glsl`
  - `src/client/shaders/milkyway.{vert,frag}.glsl`

  Each pair needs:
  - **vert pars**: `#include <common>` + `#include <logdepthbuf_pars_vertex>`
  - **vert main** (after `gl_Position` is set): `#include <logdepthbuf_vertex>`
  - **frag pars**: `#include <common>` + `#include <logdepthbuf_pars_fragment>`
  - **frag main** (early): `#include <logdepthbuf_fragment>`

  All four pairs already use `THREE.ShaderMaterial({ glslVersion: THREE.GLSL3, … })` — three.js will inject the chunks correctly under GLSL3 (verified in `stellata.ts` and `milkyway.ts`).

# Verification

No visible change is expected. Run `npm run dev` and confirm:
1. Default view looks identical to `main`. Diff a screenshot if in doubt.
2. Warp / observe / chart mode still render correctly.
3. Molecular clouds, dust particles, milky way all draw in the right depth order with no new banding.
4. `npm run typecheck` clean; `npm test` green.

# Acceptance

- `WebGLRenderer` constructed with `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true`.
- All 8 shader files include the log-depth chunks.
- `camera.near` lowered to `1e-10`.
- No visual regressions vs. `main`.

title: Physical-scale rewrite of star sizing & close approach
description:
Rewrite the star disc / close-approach pipeline so that resolved discs match true angular geometry (`θ = 2·atan(R/d)`) and the orbit + parking distances scale with each star's physical radius.

# Why

Stellata's stated intent is *physical reality with perceptual exaggeration only where the eye demands it.* Today the close-approach pipeline breaks that contract in three places:

1. **Disc-pass size formula is non-physical.** `physSize = sizeAtRef × (uRefDistPc / dPc) × radiusFactor`, where `sizeAtRef` is a *log-mapped pixel target* — the catalogue's full radius span (white-dwarf → hypergiant) is compressed into `[2 px, 50% of viewport]` at an arbitrary reference distance of `0.005 pc`. The `1/d` falloff is correct geometry, but it's applied to a perceptually-warped base — so the on-screen ratio between Sol and Betelgeuse does **not** match `R_Sol / R_Betelgeuse`.
2. **Reference distance is locked to `controls.minDistance`.** `uRefDistPc` and the orbit floor are the same number (`DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC = 0.005 pc`). Past that floor, growth saturates instead of continuing as physics demands.
3. **Auto-park targets a uniform 10 px disc.** `TARGET_APPROACH_DISC_PX = 10` collapses the physical-radius hierarchy at warp arrival: a white dwarf and Betelgeuse arrive identical-looking. UX-coherent, physics-incoherent.

Background-star calibration (point-source perceptual blob driven by arcsec-per-pixel × exaggeration K) is **correct** and must remain pixel-identical after this change. Only the resolved disc and the close-range floors change. Future work (exoplanets, solar-system mode) needs this physical foundation.

# Approach (summary)

For a star of physical radius `R` (pc) at camera distance `d` (pc):

```
θ_rad      = 2 · atan(R · radiusFactor / d)        (true angular diameter)
physSizePx = θ_rad · viewport.y / fov_y_rad        (pixel diameter, screen-aligned quad)
```

`pxSize = max(appSize, physSize)` continues to give the natural crossover between the perceptual blob and the physical disc. Variable pulsation multiplies `R` (`R_eff = R · radiusFactor`). The `vPhysRatio` disc-pass / glow-pass split is now driven by *real* angular size.

Closest manual zoom (90% of fill-the-viewport):
```
ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.9
fov_minor = min(fov_x, fov_y)
d_min(star) = R / tan(ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION · fov_minor / 2)
```

Auto-park (warp arrival, observe-exit):
```
TARGET_PARK_FRACTION = 0.10
d_park(star) = R / tan(TARGET_PARK_FRACTION · fov_minor / 2)
```

Z-buffer precision is the critical enabler: at d_min for Sol-class stars (`R ≈ 2.25e-8 pc`, `d_min ≈ 5e-8 pc`), standard depth precision collapses. Phase 0 enables `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` on the renderer and adds `<logdepthbuf>` chunks to every custom GLSL3 ShaderMaterial.

# Phasing

Tracked as two child tasks:
- **Phase 0** — Enable log depth buffer + add `<logdepthbuf>` chunks to all shaders. Pure prerequisite, no visual change expected.
- **Phase 1** — Replace disc formula, orbit floor, parking distance. Depends on Phase 0.

# Out of scope

- Star colour / luminosity-class softness (untouched).
- Background-star calibration (untouched — confirmed correct).
- Chart mode disc sizing (`uMonochrome > 0.5` path is independent).
- `star-tuning.ts` debug panel (its sliders don't expose the removed uniforms).

# Acceptance

1. Background stars pixel-identical to current `main` from default view (naked-eye preset).
2. Resolved-disc ratio between Betelgeuse and Sirius A at equal `d/R` matches `R_Betel / R_Sirius`.
3. Manual zoom to a focused star grows the disc until ~90% of viewport minor axis, then stops. Works for white dwarfs, Sol-class, and supergiants.
4. Warp arrival: every star arrives at 10% of viewport minor axis. Parking distance scales with R.
5. FOV `+`/`-` while at zoom limit recomputes the orbit floor live.
6. Disc mask, focus ring, distance-vector chevron all track the new disc size correctly.
7. No z-fighting at d_min for Sol-class stars; no banding artefacts in the milky-way / cloud / dust-particle layers.
8. `npm run typecheck` clean; `npm test` (244 regression tests) green.

# Plan file

Full plan at `~/.claude/plans/resume-planning-from-users-alexm-claude-reflective-wombat.md`.

title: Design: rework settings panel to be contextual to camera mode (nav vs observe)
description:
The right-side settings panel today shows a single static control set regardless of which mode the user is in. Navigate and observe expose different affordances and want different things at the user's fingertips, so the panel ends up cluttered for both. This is a usability/UX issue: the user wants ready access to mode-relevant controls without scanning the whole panel.

Deliverable: a written design spec (in docs/ or attached to this issue) covering, at minimum:
- For each mode, which controls are primary vs secondary vs hidden.
- Layout: are mode-specific sections inserted/removed, or shown via tab/segmented control, or via collapse-by-default rules?
- Behaviour on mode-switch — animation, scroll position, collapse memory.
- Are there observe-mode controls that don't yet exist and should be added (e.g. FOV preset buttons, label-density slider) — flag them for separate feature issues, do not add scope here.
- Reverse-sync implications: today the panel reads back from Stellata.getFilter()/state; the design must keep that contract intact.

Critical files to read before designing:

- src/client/panel-layout.ts — top-level + per-group collapse behaviour the redesign must respect or replace.
- src/client/mode-toggle.ts — the navigate↔observe pill that triggers the mode change the panel needs to react to.

- docs/ui-and-controls.md — documents existing layout containers, magnitude presets/override flags, CSS gotchas ([hidden] specificity, backdrop-filter stacking contexts).

title: Mobile Safari chrome polish + Add-to-Home-Screen PWA path
description:
Goal: a deliberately-mobile-aware chrome layout in iOS Safari, plus a polished Add-to-Home-Screen (PWA standalone) path for users who want full-bleed.

WHAT WAS LEARNED (from PR #6, abandoned):
- Browser iOS Safari does NOT let arbitrary content paint behind the URL bar while the URL bar is at full size, regardless of `viewport-fit=cover` + `100lvh` + safe-area-inset. The chrome stays opaque.
- The 'modern site' behaviour the user remembers (URL bar shrinks; canvas extends behind) only kicks in for *scrollable* pages — iOS minimises the URL bar after a user scroll. Stellata is fixed-viewport (overflow: hidden) so there's no scroll, so the URL bar never minimises, so full-bleed never engages.
- Workarounds (artificially-scrollable body, programmatic scroll-to-1px) are fragile across iOS versions and fight our gesture-capturing canvas.
- Standalone PWA (Add to Home Screen) has none of these limits — no URL bar at all, full-bleed by default.
- We did achieve correct sizing of the GL buffer relative to the layout viewport via documentElement.clientWidth/clientHeight (vs window.innerWidth/innerHeight). That code is in PR #6's history if we want to recover it, but the user opted to revert and start fresh under a re-scoped issue.

NEW SCOPE:
1. Polish what we can in browser Safari without chasing impossible full-bleed:
   - Confirm + fix scale-bar / settings panel positioning so they sit cleanly above the URL bar (use `100vh - 100dvh` / safe-area-inset-bottom thoughtfully, not sum).
   - Add the meta tags that improve the experience: `viewport-fit=cover`, `apple-mobile-web-app-capable`, `apple-mobile-web-app-status-bar-style=black-translucent`, `theme-color`.
   - Avoid the `100% / inner*` buffer-vs-CSS stretch: size the GL buffer to the layout viewport (documentElement.clientWidth/clientHeight) so stars don't squash on iOS.
2. Make Add-to-Home-Screen produce a clean standalone experience:
   - Manifest.json with name, short_name, theme_color, background_color, icons (192/512 PNG), display: standalone.
   - Apple touch icon link in <head>.
   - First-launch behaviour in standalone mode (no URL bar; full-bleed by default).

DEPENDS ON 47c.3 (design-decision) — the gating-vs-recommend question changes how aggressive this work is. If we hard-block phone viewports, this issue shrinks to 'just AtHS polish'. If we soft-warn, this is the scaffolding for a deliberately mobile-aware chrome layout.

OUT OF SCOPE: touch gestures (split into 47c.4); any layout work for the settings panel itself (covered by the existing panel-rework design issue 1yu).
notes:
Implementation landed on claude/add-mobile-and-ux-issues:
- index.html: viewport-fit=cover, theme-color, apple-mobile-web-app-capable, status-bar-style=black-translucent
- styles.css: html/body height -> 100dvh; #scene + .overlay sized to 100dvw/100dvh so the CSS rect tracks the iOS visual viewport (matching the GL buffer that's already sized to window.innerHeight); modal max-height + kb-modal margin migrated to dvh; the four edge-anchored chrome containers (.ui-top, .ui-top-left, .ui-bottom, .warp-btn) wrapped with max(<n>px, env(safe-area-inset-*)) / calc(<n>px + env(safe-area-inset-*)) so they clear the notch / Dynamic Island / home indicator without changing desktop spacing.

title: Mobile experience: make Stellata workable on phones / small touch devices
description:
Stellata today is built and tuned for desktop — TrackballControls with mouse + wheel, a fixed right-side settings panel, modals sized for desktop viewports, and a render budget tuned for discrete GPUs. The result on phones is degraded and in places unusable: gestures don't map cleanly (no pinch-to-zoom, two-finger roll is desktop-Safari-only by design), the panel covers the canvas, modals overflow, and performance has not been characterised on mobile chips.

Goal: make Stellata a deliberate, usable mobile experience without compromising the desktop product. Concrete subtasks are deferred to the audit issue below — touch gestures, responsive panel layout, modal sizing, mobile-perf characterisation, and any catalog/dust downscaling decisions are the likely categories but should be confirmed by the audit before being broken out.

Out of scope (for now): a separate mobile build, native app packaging, replacing TrackballControls wholesale on desktop.

Critical files (for orientation when subtasks are scoped):
- src/client/controls.ts — right-side settings panel layout/widgets.
- src/client/stellata.ts — TrackballControls wiring + camera state machine.
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — drag/yaw/pitch + wheel FOV (desktop-tuned).
- src/client/styles.css, src/client/index.html — viewport meta, layout containers.
- docs/camera-modes.md, docs/ui-and-controls.md — current desktop assumptions (two-finger roll = Safari-only, panel reverse-sync, etc.).

This epic closes only when its subtasks are all closed.

title: Further compress the view URL parameter
description:
The `?v=` blob (schema v2, in `src/client/url-state.ts`) pays a fixed 12-byte cost per vec3 (`cam`, `tgt`, `up`) whenever **any single component** diverges from its default. A camera at `[0, 0, 3.7]` — the common case for a near-Sol orbit on the z-axis — burns 8 bytes encoding two zero-valued floats.

Worked example: `https://stellata.xyz/?v=AgEAAAAAAAAAAAAAPtRsQA` decodes to:

- byte 0: `0x02` — version 2
- bytes 1–3: `0x000001` — presence mask, only bit 0 (`cam`) set
- bytes 4–15: cam float32 LE × 3 = `[0.0, 0.0, ~3.7004]`

→ 16 bytes, 22 base64url chars. 8 of those bytes (≈11 chars) are zero floats.

Two compression opportunities:

1. **Per-component vec3 encoding.** Only emit floats for components that diverge from the per-component default. The defaults already vary per vec3 (`cam=[0,0,30]` navigate / `[0,0,0]` observe; `tgt=[0,0,0]`; `up=[0,1,0]`), so a per-component compare is the natural extension of the existing whole-vec3 default check at `url-state.ts:614-623`.

2. **Varint presence mask.** The 24-bit mask is often near-zero in its high bytes (the example has `0x00 0x00` for bytes 2–3). LEB128 (7 bits payload + continuation bit) collapses single-bit masks to 1 byte instead of 3.

The exponent-notation idea from the original sketch (encode `1.24` in fewer bytes than full float32) is harder to land cleanly without losing round-trip exactness. float32 is already a binary-exponent format; shrinking to float16 trades ~3 sig-figs of precision and breaks at galactic distances. Keeping it as out-of-scope for the first pass — possibly worth revisiting just for the `up` vector, which is always normalised and ≤1.0 per component.

Headline impact for the example URL: 22 chars → ~12 chars (≈45% shorter), with no precision loss.
design:
# Schema v3 (in `src/client/url-state.ts`)

Bump `SCHEMA_VERSION` from 2 to 3. Keep `FIELDS_V1`/`decodeV1` and `FIELDS_V2`/`decodeV2` frozen verbatim — same pattern already in use. Add `FIELDS_V3` and `decodeV3`. The auto-upgrade rewrite at `applyFromUrl:778` already triggers when `decoded.version !== SCHEMA_VERSION`, so legacy URLs in the address bar will be silently rewritten to v3 on next load.

## Change 1 — per-component vec3 encoding (primary win)

Replace `vec3Field` for v3 with a variant that emits a 1-byte sub-mask prefix:

```
[1 byte: lower 3 bits = which components diverge from default]
[for each set bit: 4-byte f32 LE]
```

Per-vec3 byte cost (vs. v2's flat 12 bytes whenever the field is present):

| diverging components | v3 bytes  | v2 bytes | Δ  |
|----------------------|-----------|----------|-----|
| 0 (field omitted)    | 0         | 0        | 0   |
| 1                    | 1 + 4 = 5 | 12       | −7  |
| 2                    | 1 + 8 = 9 | 12       | −3  |
| 3                    | 1 + 12 = 13 | 12     | +1  |

`isPresent` returns true iff any component diverges from the per-component default — same trigger as today, just per-component. Components with bit clear fill in from the per-component default at decode time.

### Wrinkle: `cam` default depends on mode

The encoder already handles this at `currentStateOf:614` (`camDefault = mode === 'observe' ? [0,0,0] : DEFAULT_CAM`). The decoder doesn't know mode at the point it decodes `cam`, because mode comes from the `flags` field (bit 13). Two options:

- **(a) Two-pass decode.** Decode all fields, then post-fix `cam` defaults using the now-known mode. Cheap (one extra branch after the field loop) but couples decode order.
- **(b) Always treat cam default as `[0,0,30]` on the wire.** Simpler, but sacrifices the savings on observe-mode shares where `cam.z=0`.

**Recommend (a).** The post-decode fix is a single `if (mask & cam_bit && view.cam) { ... }` check after the loop, reading `view.mode` from the already-decoded `flags`. No structural change to the field-loop machinery.

### Defaults table

Hoist a per-key default table at module scope so encoder and decoder share one source of truth:

```ts
const VEC3_DEFAULTS_NAVIGATE: Record<'cam'|'tgt'|'up', readonly [number,number,number]> = {
  cam: [0, 0, 30], tgt: [0, 0, 0], up: [0, 1, 0],
};
const VEC3_DEFAULTS_OBSERVE_CAM: readonly [number, number, number] = [0, 0, 0];
```

## Change 2 — varint presence mask (secondary win, separate commit)

Replace the fixed 3-byte mask with LEB128:

- 7 bits payload + 1 continuation bit per byte
- Mask `0x000001` → 1 byte (saves 2)
- Mask `0x000800` → 2 bytes (saves 1)
- Mask `0x080001` → 3 bytes (no change)

`encodeBlob` writes the varint after the version byte; `decodeV3` reads the varint and tracks the resulting offset before stepping through fields. No changes to per-field encode/decode contracts.

Land as a follow-up commit on top of Change 1 so the v3 round-trip suite already exists when the mask format moves.

## Test plan

`src/client/url-state.test.ts` already has byte-length assertions and round-trip suites. Extend with:

- v3 round-trip block — copy the pattern from the existing vec3 / scalar / focus / POI suites, but assert tighter byte budgets (e.g. `cam=[0,0,3.7]` blob ≤ 9 bytes, ≤ 12 base64url chars).
- v2-decode regression block — clone the v1-decode regression at `url-state.test.ts:421+`. Encode a known view via the v2 path (need a tiny test helper that runs `FIELDS_V2`-flavoured encode), feed to `decodeBlob`, assert the same `view` falls out and `version === 2`.
- Component-default fill tests — `roundtrip({ cam: [0, 0, 3.7] })` returns `cam: [0, 0, 3.7]` (decoder fills the omitted x/y from default). `roundtrip({ tgt: [0, 0, 0] })` omits the field entirely. Mode-aware cam default: encode with `mode: 'observe'` and `cam: [0, 0, 0]` → field omitted; with navigate and `cam: [0, 0, 0]` → cam present (because navigate default is `[0, 0, 30]`).
- Update the empty-blob length assertion at `url-state.test.ts:18` if Change 2 lands (varint mask shrinks empty to 2 bytes / 3 chars).

## Out of scope

- float16 / varint-mantissa float encoding for cam/tgt. Addressed in description — defer.
- float16 for `up` only (always normalised, ≤1.0). Tempting; revisit once Change 1 is landed and we have measurements.
- Compressing other fields (`spect` is a 9-bit mask, can't shrink; `dmin`/`dmax` u16 already maps to slider step count, u8 would lose granularity).

## Files touched

- `src/client/url-state.ts` — primary
- `src/client/url-state.test.ts` — regression coverage
acceptance_criteria:
- Existing v1 and v2 URLs continue to decode unchanged (regression suite enforces this).
- `?v=` blob for `cam = [0, 0, 3.7]` with no other state drops from 22 base64url chars to ≤14 chars (and ≤12 chars after Change 2).
- Default state still produces no `?v=` query param at all.
- Fully-populated vec3s (every component non-default) grow by at most 1 byte per vec3 — accepted trade.
- Round-trip exactness preserved for every v3 field (no precision loss vs. v2 within the schema's already-quantised scalar grid).
- Vitest suite extended with v3 round-trips and a v2-decode regression block matching the existing v1-regression coverage at `url-state.test.ts:421+`.
- Address bar auto-rewrites legacy URLs to v3 on load (existing `applyFromUrl` upgrade pattern, no new code needed).
- Mode-dependent `cam` default (`[0,0,30]` navigate / `[0,0,0]` observe) handled correctly on encode and decode; covered by tests.
