title: ATHYG reduced_m12 catalog upgrade
description:
**Goal:** populate the resolved-discrete star layer at intermediate travel distances (50 pc – ~kpc from Sol), where `classic_ids` runs out of entries because faint distant stars rarely have classical designations.

Complements Phase 5. Phase 5 fills the diffuse glow at distance; this phase fills the point sources at intermediate range. Together they make travel away from Sol feel populated at every scale.

**Data source:** ATHYG v3.3 `reduced_m12` subset — apparent magnitude ≤ 12, ~2M stars. Same source repository as the current `classic_ids`: https://codeberg.org/astronexus/athyg

**What changes:**
- `data/athyg_33_classic_ids.csv` → replace with `data/athyg_33_reduced_m12.csv` (~400 MB CSV, LFS).
- `scripts/build-catalog.ts` reads the bigger CSV. Same field mapping; same 40-byte stride; same sort-by-absmag pipeline.
- Output `public/catalog.bin` grows from ~12 MB to ~80 MB (~20 MB gzipped).
- `search-index.json` content unchanged — only stars with classical identifiers go in the search index. The added ~1.7M nameless stars contribute to rendering only.
- GCVS variability cross-match picks up significantly more variables (more HIP/HD entries are present in the bigger catalog).
- Geometric binary inference runs over more stars; expect substantially more pairs found, but the `BINARY_MAX_SEP_PC = 0.005 pc` threshold keeps the count visually relevant.

**Watch out for:**
- Initial catalog download grows from ~3 MB gzipped to ~20 MB gzipped. Significant for first-load on slow connections. Worth measuring whether progressive load (render available chunks while the rest streams) is needed.
- 2M instanced quads at 60 fps needs profiling on modest hardware (integrated GPUs, mobile). The two-pass split already handles overdraw efficiently, but instance-attribute memory grows linearly. If there's a perf cliff, fall back to `reduced_m10` (~350k stars) as a smaller cut.
- `classic_ids` ⊂ `reduced_m12` in practice (stars with classical IDs are almost all brighter than mag 12 from Earth), but verify a few iconic targets (Proxima, Barnard's Star, Sol-itself) survive the switch.
- Constellation index assignment per star uses the AT-HYG `con` column unchanged — works for the bigger catalog out of the box.

**Estimate:** half-day for the build pipeline change + verification runs. Most of the time goes to catalog regeneration and perf testing.
notes: 2026-05-02 — design principle decided: the apparent-magnitude cutoff of the chosen catalog subset (m11 or m12) becomes the **model's magnitude floor** — the dimmest threshold for *all* objects in the model, not just stars. Anything dimmer than this floor is excluded from the model regardless of category (variable stars, nebulae, projected distant DSOs, etc.). This makes the magnitude UI control coherent across all object types and gives a clean contract: 'show me everything brighter than mag X' applies uniformly. The decision in stellata-cns.1 (m11 vs m12) therefore sets the model's floor, not just the star catalog cutoff.

title: Per-star dust slab fallback for sightlines exiting the Edenhofer voxel volume
description:
**Goal:** correct per-star extinction at the boundary of the high-fidelity dust voxel volume.

`star.vert.glsl` raymarches camera→star through the Edenhofer voxel volume only (~1.25 kpc AABB at world origin). Lines of sight that exit the AABB get zero attenuation beyond — wrong direction at the edge. A star sitting just outside the voxel cube renders unattenuated even though there's intervening dust along the sightline.

**Fix:** borrow the analytical dust profile already shipped in `milkyway.frag.glsl` (`analyticalDustDensity` = `exp(-(R-R₀)/L) × exp(-|z|/H)` with scale height h ≈ 125 pc, evaluated in Galactic frame). Integrate it over the segment of the sightline outside the voxel AABB. Per-star extinction then has a sensible value everywhere — high fidelity inside the cube, smooth analytical fallback outside.

Lift the function into a shared shader include (or duplicate constants) so `star.vert.glsl` and `milkyway.frag.glsl` agree on the analytical model.

**Files:**
- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — call the shared slab function beyond AABB
- Possibly a new shader include for shared analytical-dust constants/function

**Watch out for:**
- Slab evaluation requires ICRS→Galactic rotation matching Phase 5. Keep it as one shared shader function so both phases use identical maths.
- Calibration: the analytical profile and Edenhofer voxel values should be roughly continuous at the AABB boundary (no sudden brightness jump as a star crosses the edge). Verify visually.

**Estimate:** ~2 hours.

**Note:** this issue used to bundle a UI status chip + dataset-boundary mesh under the same scope. Those have been extracted into the artificial-visualisation cluster (`stellata-io3`) since they're a different conceptual category — opt-in visualisation of dataset metadata, not a 'you're in degraded mode' indicator.

title: Radcliffe Wave spine
description:
**Goal:** glowing polyline showing the ~3 kpc oscillating gas wave that passes near the Sun. Famous nearby structure, narratively striking, very small data.

**Data sources:**
- **Alves et al. 2020** (Nature, original discovery).
- **Konietzka et al. 2024** — oscillation phase data.
- Catherine Zucker's GitHub / interactive gallery again is the practical entry point for the spine point list (~100 points).

**Implementation sketch:**
- Read ~100 (x, y, z) spine points from source. Tiny — kilobytes.
- Render as a `Line2` (Three.js fat line helper) with emissive shader, optionally colour-coded by oscillation phase from Konietzka 2024.
- Label "Radcliffe Wave" at midpoint of the spine.
- UI toggle.
- Bonus: when toggled on, highlight catalogued molecular clouds that are part of the wave (cross-ref against Phase 3a's cloud catalog).

**Watch out for:**
- Coordinate frame conversion (Galactic → ICRS).
- The wave extends across ~3 kpc, well past the 1.25 kpc Edenhofer volume; the spine itself is fine to render outside the dust grid, it just won't have associated dust at its extreme ends.

**Estimate:** 2–4 hours.

title: Local Bubble shell mesh
description:
**Goal:** translucent surface mesh of the Local Bubble (low-density ISM cavity surrounding the Sun, ~200 pc radius). Subtle by default, toggleable for emphasis. Gives the user immediate context that the Sun sits *inside* a bubble.

**Data sources:**
- **O'Neill, Zucker, Goodman & Edenhofer 2024** ("The Local Bubble Is a Local Chimney", ApJ 973, 136 / arXiv:2407.18238). The paper should have a data availability section; if not, check Catherine Zucker's GitHub: https://github.com/catherinezucker
- **Zucker et al. 2022** (Nature, "Star formation near the Sun is driven by the expansion of the Local Bubble") — earlier 3D mesh. Also linked from her interactive 3D gallery at https://catherinezucker.github.io/

**Implementation sketch:**
- Source format likely OBJ/PLY/HDF5 mesh. Convert at preprocessor time to a tiny binary (vertex positions + face indices). Few thousand vertices typically.
- Three.js: `THREE.Mesh` with `THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({ transparent: true, opacity: 0.05, side: DoubleSide, blending: AdditiveBlending })`.
- Subtle inner/outer gradient via vertex colours (front-facing vs back-facing) so users can perceive 3D shape.
- Label "Local Bubble" at the centroid, similar to constellation labels.
- UI toggle.

**Watch out for:**
- Coordinate frame — likely Galactic; rotate to ICRS on ingest.
- Mesh might be in different units (kpc vs pc); verify scale.
- Front-vs-back face ordering matters for the gradient effect.

**Estimate:** 3–5 hours.

title: Nebulae catalogs (PN, RNe, H II, SNR)
description:
**Goal:** classical named nebulae (Orion Nebula, Ring Nebula, Helix, Veil, Horsehead, etc.) as discrete blob objects with type-based colour coding and search-bar entries.

**Data sources** (filter to within ~3 kpc to match the dust volume):
- **Strasbourg-ESO Catalogue of Galactic Planetary Nebulae** (Acker et al. 1992, updated): VizieR `V/84` or HEASARC `PLNEBULAE`. ~1,500 PNe with up to four independent distance estimates each.
- **Merged Catalog of Reflection Nebulae** (Magakian 2003): VizieR `J/A+A/399/141` or HEASARC `REFNEBULAE`. 913 reflection nebulae; cross-match the illuminating star to ATHYG via HD/HIP for distance.
- **Sharpless H II catalog**: VizieR `VII/20`. 313 entries; pair with Avedisova's "Catalogue of Star-Forming Regions" (VizieR `V/115`) for derived distances since the original catalog has none.
- **SIMBAD TAP query** for unified all-types fetch: object types `PN`, `RNe`, `HII`, `SNR` with a distance cut, via `astroquery.simbad`.

**Per-nebula data:** ID, common name, type, RA/Dec/distance → xyz, angular size → physical radius, illuminating star (for RNe).

**Rendering — this is where raymarching IS appropriate.** Each nebula occupies a small AABB (typically 1–50 pc), so per-nebula raymarching with 16–32 samples is dense in voxel-space and produces smooth volumetrics without the aliasing that killed the all-sky fog. See `memory/feedback_raymarch_for_nebulae.md` for context. Recover the fog shader pattern from git history at commit `5ad5444` if useful — it's a clip-space fullscreen triangle, ray-AABB slab intersection, fixed-step march with log-encoded density decode.

**Type-based visuals:**
- H II regions — warm reddish-pink, soft sphere with density falloff
- Planetary nebulae — greenish-teal, often shell-like; small central point + thin shell
- Reflection nebulae — bluish, irregular blob biased toward the illuminating star's colour (look up via ATHYG cross-ref)
- Supernova remnants — filamentary; pink→blue gradient by age

**No photographs.** All visuals procedural, matching the rest of the app's aesthetic.

**Watch out for:**
- Many PNe have only rough distance estimates; drop entries with no parsed distance.
- Filter to ATHYG-relevant volume — most distant PNe (>5 kpc) won't render usefully and clutter search.
- Procedural shape variation per type — without it, every nebula is a blob and the type encoding gets lost.

**Estimate:** 1–2 days for catalogs + ingest + rendering. The volumetric raymarching for the per-nebula effect is ~half the work.

title: Toliman at min distance: dark halo ring around disc — likely companion overlap
description:
Visual artifact observed when zooming to minimum distance on Toliman (α Cen B, HIP 71681) in navigate mode.

# What you see

A single bright disc fills the lower-left of the viewport (per user screenshot). Just outside the bright core is a clearly-visible darker gray ring/halo, then the falloff resumes normally. The halo reads as a separate object's outline rendered behind/inside the focused star's disc.

# What's happening (working theory)

α Cen is a tight system: A (Rigil Kentaurus) and B (Toliman) sit ~24 AU apart on average — within the 0.005 pc geometric-binary radius. At minimum-distance focus on Toliman, the camera ends up inside or extremely close to the *other* component's render volume, so we're rendering the companion's disc geometry on top of (or coincident with) the focused star.

The dark ring is consistent with the *companion's* core depth-mask being written under the focused star's outer falloff: the depth mask occludes the additive glow contribution from the other star, leaving a dark band where one star's mask sits inside the other's halo.

# Likely resolves under stellata-a7d

stellata-a7d (Physical-scale rewrite of star sizing & close approach) replaces the close-approach pipeline with true angular geometry. Under that model, two stars 24 AU apart at any reasonable zoom are spatially resolved — they won't share screen pixels at min-distance focus the way they do today. So this bug should fall out for free once a7d lands.

# Verify before fixing

- Confirm the dark ring is the companion's core mask (toggle the companion off / hide its disc and see if the halo disappears).
- If a7d is months out and this is still annoying, a smaller fix is to push the focused star's disc-pass forward in depth or skip rendering the companion when it's within the focused star's screen extent.

# Repro

Focus 'Toliman' in navigate mode, manual-zoom all the way in until the disc fills ~½ the viewport's smaller dimension. The dark halo ring is visible just outside the bright core.

title: Roll-up: small comment/WHY/dead-code cleanups from code review
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84). Roll-up of small findings worth fixing in one cleanup pass; each is 1-3 lines and low-risk.

**Comment / WHY gaps**
- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:201-216 — magic constant `VAR_TROUGH_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.2` has no comment. Add a one-line WHY ("perceptual floor below which a variable reads as 'disappeared' rather than 'dimmed'").
- scripts/catalog-pure.ts:34 — comment says "C/S/W (cool carbon)" but code also matches N and R; extend to "C/S/W/N/R".
- scripts/catalog-pure.ts:113-123 — `interpolate` fallthrough is unreachable in practice; comment as such or assert.
- src/client/galactic-coords.ts:36 — note that `galZ` ends up parallel-to-but-not-equal-NGP after re-orthogonalisation.
- src/client/catalog-loader.ts:130 — note "byte 37: padding" after the amplitude read.
- src/client/catalog-loader.ts:144 — extend the existing nameOffset=0 sentinel comment with "(allows nameOffset=0 to mean 'no name')".

**Small dead/redundant**
- src/client/main.ts:74 — declare `window.stellata` in src/client/globals.d.ts instead of `as unknown as` cast.
- src/client/url-state.ts:142-144 — `fixed(n)` returns two functions both ignoring args; signatures are misleading.
- src/client/url-state.ts:32 + src/client/stellata.ts:73 — `ALL_SPECT_MASK` defined twice; export from one and import in the other.
- src/client/warp-button.ts:11 — `as unknown as SVGGElement` is a type lie; type as `Element` (no cast needed for `addEventListener`).
- src/client/observe-controls.ts:62-65 — fields could be `readonly`.

**Shader micro-wins**
- src/client/shaders/cloud.frag.glsl:29 — replace 4-component dot product with `abs(normalize(vNormalView).z)`.
- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:203-217, 291-294 — `baseSize0`, `logSpan0`, `logRatio0`, `sizeAtRef0` are computed twice (variability branch and non-variable branch); compute once outside.

**Galactic disc**
- src/client/galactic-disc.ts:131-156 — `makeRing` allocates a fresh `THREE.LineBasicMaterial` per ring (6 rings, all visually identical). Use one shared material; opacity update writes once.

**Star tuning panel**
- src/client/star-tuning.ts:13 + src/client/milkyway-tuning.ts:97 — closure-captured initial values become stale if `setStarRenderParams` / `setReddeningRGB` is called from elsewhere (URL state, future presets). Either document the no-reverse-sync, or read live state on each `onChange`.

**Style**
- src/client/molecular-clouds.ts:48-50 — `meshIndex: Map<string, number>` keyed by `mesh.uuid`. Store `mesh.userData.cloudIdx = i` at construction; raycast reads `hits[0].object.userData.cloudIdx`. Drops the Map.
- src/client/poi-overlay.ts:373 — `catalog: { hip: Uint32Array }` structural-typed param hides dependency; use `Catalog`.
- src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts:99-112 — `cTapTimer` non-null sentinel for "first tap pending" reads better as `pendingFirstCTap` boolean alongside the timer.

Severity: LOW/MEDIUM (cleanup pass; safe single-line fixes).

title: Remove dead showMolecularClouds plumbing post-shelving
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

`FilterState.showMolecularClouds` was removed from the user UI when the molecular-cloud overlay was shelved for v1.0 (CLAUDE.md), but the field remains threaded through:

- src/client/stellata.ts:55, 334 — interface and DEFAULT_FILTER (always false)
- src/client/stellata.ts:1180-1189 — `pickCloud` checks the flag (always-false branch)
- src/client/stellata.ts:2693, 2739 — `clouds.update(worldOffset, false)` is always called with literal false
- src/client/main.ts:286 — `pickStar`-then-`pickCloud` chain runs through dead path

Fix: One of:
- (a) Remove the flag and the threading through. URL bit 2 stays reserved with a one-line comment.
- (b) Keep the flag but mark with a single comment "dead until clouds layer is re-enabled" and remove the conditional pluming so `clouds.update()` takes no arg.

Either way, drop the `pickCloud` invocation from main.ts since it's currently unreachable.

Severity: MEDIUM (cleanup; eliminates real dead code paths).

title: build-catalog: type CSV rows + SearchEntry + dedupe GCVS pipe-reader
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

scripts/build-catalog.ts uses untyped Records throughout:

1. **CSV row access** — `csv-parse` runs with `cast: false` and rows accessed as `row.x0`, `row.absmag`, `row.spect` etc. Every column is implicitly `any`. A typo'd column name yields `undefined` and silently corrupts the binary.

   Fix: Declare `interface AthygRow { x0: string; y0: string; z0: string; absmag: string; ci: string; spect: string; con: string; ... }` and cast the parser stream once.

2. **Search-index entries** (line 758-769) — built as `Record<string, string | number>` with stringly-keyed shorthand fields (`p`, `b`, `f`, `c`, `s`, `i`, `hip`, `hd`, `hr`, `gl`). The keys are a public wire contract with src/client/search.ts; one typo here ships a broken index.

   Fix: `interface SearchEntry { i: number; p?: string; b?: string; f?: number; c?: number; s?: string; hip?: number; hd?: number; hr?: number; gl?: string; }`. Build with that type to catch drift between writer and reader.

3. **Related**: scripts/build-catalog.ts:184-214 (`parseGcvsMain`) and 216-256 (`parseGcvsCrossref`) both implement: existsSync → readFileSync → split lines → trim-skip → split on `|`. Extract `function* readPipeDelimited(path)` yielding trimmed-field arrays.

Severity: MEDIUM (type-safety + DRY in build path).

title: Catalog binary format: extract shared RECORD_LAYOUT constants + flag-bit registry
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

The binary catalog format constants (`RECORD_SIZE = 44`, `HEADER_SIZE = 32`, `NO_COMPANION = 0xffffffff`) are duplicated between scripts/build-catalog.ts and scripts/verify-catalog.ts:11. The byte-offset map exists only as scattered comments at scripts/build-catalog.ts:708-714 and 727; e.g. `setUint8(off + 36, ampUnits)`, `setUint16(off + 38, periodUnits)` are bare numeric literals.

A single change to the layout requires touching two files plus the runtime reader (src/client/catalog-loader.ts). Drift between writer and reader silently ships corrupt data.

Fix:
- Extract `const RECORD_LAYOUT = { x: 0, y: 4, z: 8, absmag: 12, ci: 16, physRadius: 20, companion: 24, nameOffset: 28, spectClass: 32, lumClass: 33, conIndex: 34, flags: 35, ampUnits: 36, period: 38, hip: 40, SIZE: 44 }` to a shared module (e.g. `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` or new `scripts/binary-format.ts`).
- Import from build-catalog.ts, verify-catalog.ts, and src/client/catalog-loader.ts.
- Use the named offsets in DataView reads/writes.

Bonus: a single canonical record-layout block-comment listing every byte (used and reserved) so adding flag bit `0x08` later doesn't collide silently. Currently flag bits `0x08`, `0x20`, `0x40`, `0x80` are unused with no documented reservation.

Severity: MEDIUM (no current bug, but it's the most realistic vector for a future corrupt-binary regression).

title: Stellata event-bus consolidation: replace eight on*Handlers arrays with typed bus
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/stellata.ts has eight `on*Handlers` arrays and matching `on*Change` getters / `fire*Change` firers (focus, cloud focus, frame, camera mode, pois, vector, filter, ...). ~150 lines of mechanical boilerplate.

Fix: Replace with a single typed `EventBus<EventMap>` with `on(name, handler)` / `emit(name, payload)`. Concrete payload types follow through to subscribers in url-state.ts, controls.ts, etc. Eliminates duplication and tightens types at every callsite.

Optional sub-improvement: dedupe handlers on register (Set instead of Array) — currently nothing prevents double-registration.

Severity: MEDIUM (DRY — biggest single readability win in stellata.ts).

title: Unify SearchBox and constellation-typeahead; share escapeHtml
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

Two related cleanups around search and the typeahead:

1. **`escapeHtml` duplicated** — defined verbatim in search.ts:557-563 and constellation-typeahead.ts:143-149. Move to a shared util and import.

2. **Two parallel implementations of the same UI** — `SearchBox` class in search.ts and standalone `bindConstellationTypeahead` in constellation-typeahead.ts are nearly identical: same input/results/active-row pattern, same listeners, same race-deferred blur, same render markup (`<span class="sub">`). The typeahead is essentially a `SearchBox` with a different filter function.

Fix: Have `bindConstellationTypeahead` instantiate `SearchBox` with a constellation-specific filter and item renderer. Removes ~150 LOC and a parallel implementation. (This is removing duplication, not adding abstraction.)

Severity: MEDIUM (DRY + reduces drift risk).

title: Arrow shaft + screen-direction helpers shared by hud-overlay and poi-overlay
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/hud-overlay.ts (Sol/GC arrows) and src/client/poi-overlay.ts (observe-mode pinned-star arrows) implement near-identical algorithms:

- aux-step calculation: `auxStepW = (ARROW_PIXEL_LENGTH * dist) / focalPx` with `focalPx = h / (2 * tan(fov*π/360))`
- screen-direction derivation from world-space dir + camera (with target-projection fallback)
- shaft-shrink + `buildArrowSvgPath` + label-clamp block (~80 lines duplicated almost verbatim between hud-overlay.ts:283-318 and poi-overlay.ts:328-352)

The shared utility module `arrow-path.ts` already exists for the SVG path builder; the screen-direction and aux-step math are the natural next thing to live there.

Fix: Extract:
- `screenDirectionFromWorldDir(camera, origin, dir, target, w, h)` → arrow-path.ts
- `renderArrowAndLabel(path, bg|null, label, ...)` for the shaft+label block

Migrate both overlays to use the helpers.

Severity: MEDIUM (DRY — bug-fix risk: changes today require touching two places).

title: perf-hud: cache histogram spans, drop unused Stellata arg, single-pass row build
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/perf-hud.ts has two issues:

1. **Per-tick array thrash** (line 147): `Array.from(sections.keys()).filter(...).map(...).sort(...).slice(0,8)` allocates 4+ arrays at 5Hz. Single-pass build would suffice.

2. **innerHTML rebuild** (line 180): `panelEl.innerHTML = ...` reparses the entire HUD HTML, including 60 histogram spans, 5 times per second when the HUD is open.

3. **Unused parameter**: `installPerfHud(stellata: Stellata)` (line 81) doesn't use the parameter; it can be dropped along with the import at line 1 and the call site in debug.ts:64.

Fix: Build histogram bars as cached span elements once; update only `style.height` and `style.background` per tick. Build the rows array in one loop. Drop the unused Stellata argument.

Severity: MEDIUM (gated behind `debug.perf()` so impact is bounded).

title: Modal dismissal helper across info-modal/brand-modal/help-modal
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

Three modal files implement essentially the same dismissal lifecycle (ESC, backdrop click, close button, keydown listener add/remove) with subtle drift:

- **info-modal.ts** uses ID-targeted listeners (`#info-backdrop`, `#info-close`).
- **brand-modal.ts** uses `[data-modal-dismiss]` selector AND uniquely couples two modals via cross-checks of `.hidden` (line 17): when closing one modal, it inspects the other to decide whether to detach the keydown listener. Adding a third modal breaks this.
- **help-modal.ts** uses `[data-modal-dismiss]` like brand.

Fix: Extract `bindModalDismiss(modal: HTMLElement, onDismiss: () => void)` that wires ESC, `[data-modal-dismiss]` clicks, and per-open keydown lifecycle (closure-scoped, so each open's listener is disposed on close). Migrate all three modals. Tag `#info-backdrop` and `#info-close` with `data-modal-dismiss` so they unify with the others.

Note: info-modal still owns its localStorage opt-in — keep that local; only the dismissal mechanics are shared.

Severity: MEDIUM (DRY win + the brand-modal cross-check is an actual fragility).

title: Chart-mode: distant DSO glyphs (galaxies, globular clusters)
description:
**Goal:** when chart mode is active, render the projected distant DSOs from `stellata-2q8` as type-coded chart glyphs at their RA/Dec — mirroring the symbology used by nebulae in `stellata-m40.2`.

**Scope:**
- Galaxies — traditional ellipse outline (oriented to angular major axis if known)
- Globular clusters — circle with crosshair (Sky Atlas 2000.0 convention)
- Other extragalactic types — fall back to galaxy ellipse or generic outline

**Constraints (from the m40 epic):**
- Chart mode is monochrome — line style and shape carry the type encoding
- Outlines should read at the same visual weight as constellation lines

**Implementation:**
- Extend `chart-mode.ts` / `chart-labels.ts` to register a DSO-overlay path
- Use the same projected-at-infinity celestial-sphere positions from `stellata-2q8`
- Symbology lookup from `stellata-m40.1`'s design reference

**Visibility:** active when chart mode is engaged. Picks up the same magnitude-floor filter that drives `stellata-2q8` in non-chart mode.

**Depends on:** `stellata-m40.1` (symbology design) + `stellata-2q8` (DSO data + base rendering).

title: Projected distant DSOs on celestial sphere (Andromeda, Messier objects, etc.)
description:
**Goal:** include the catalogued naked-eye / brighter-than-floor objects that exist *beyond* the model's spatial volume — Andromeda (M31), Triangulum (M33), distant globular clusters, other Messier galaxies — projected onto the celestial sphere at effective infinity. Filter visibility by the existing magnitude UI control.

**Why:** completes the perceptual model. A user looking at the sky should see Andromeda where Andromeda actually is, even though it's at ~780 kpc — well outside the model's volume. Computationally near-free since each object is a single point with no real 3D position to maintain.

**Scope:**
- **In:** extragalactic objects + distant globular clusters + any catalogued DSO that's *beyond the model's spatial volume* but brighter than the model's magnitude floor (see `stellata-cns` — the chosen m11 or m12 catalog cutoff defines the floor for all object categories).
- **Out:** objects already rendered in the model in 3D — M42 Orion Nebula (local SF region, comes via `stellata-9hp`), local star clusters, anything within the catalog's spatial range. Avoid double-rendering.
- **Wavelength:** human-visible spectrum only. No IR/radio sources unless their visible-band counterparts are visible.

**Magnitude semantics:** lower number = brighter. Magnitude *floor* = dimmest cutoff (highest number we render). 'Brighter than the floor' means apparent magnitude ≤ floor.

**Implementation:**
- Catalog ingest at build time. Candidates: OpenNGC (well-curated, commonly available), the Messier catalogue as a starting subset, or SIMBAD TAP for unified type-filtered fetch.
- Per-object data: name, RA/Dec, apparent magnitude, type (galaxy / globular cluster / etc.), angular size if known.
- Render: projected at fixed large distance (effectively infinity) on a celestial-sphere overlay. Direction = RA/Dec. Each object is a small glyph/disc + optional label. Type-coded visuals can match `stellata-9hp`'s nebula symbology where overlap exists (galaxies vs PNe vs globulars).
- Visibility filter: rides the existing magnitude control; objects with apparent mag > current display limit are hidden.

**Files likely touched:**
- New `scripts/build-distant-dsos.{py|ts}` for catalog ingest → `public/distant-dsos.json`
- New `src/client/distant-dsos.ts` rendering layer
- Wired into the magnitude-filter pipeline in `stellata.ts` / `controls.ts`
- Search index (`search-index.json`) gains entries with `kind: 'dso'` discriminator (parallels the cloud / nebula patterns)

**Out of scope:**
- Chart-mode treatment — handled separately via `stellata-m40` (decide there whether each type gets a chart symbol).
- True 3D positioning of these objects. They live on the celestial sphere; any user 'travel toward' them is a no-op (they recede with the camera).

**Relates to:** `stellata-cns.1` for the magnitude-floor decision that determines the catalog's apparent-mag cutoff.

**No hard dependencies — can be implemented before `cns.1` resolves; just parameterise the floor and let the magnitude UI filter.**

title: Wireframe outlines for nearby galactic structures (Magellanic Clouds, etc.)
description:
**Goal:** mirror the existing Milky Way wireframe pattern for other galactic-scale structures within the model's volume. Indicates general spatial volume of nearby satellites without trying to render them realistically.

**Candidate structures** (research scope — finalise the list during implementation):
- **Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC)** — ~50 kpc, ~14 kpc across, naked-eye visible from southern hemisphere
- **Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC)** — ~62 kpc, ~7 kpc across, naked-eye visible from southern hemisphere
- **Sagittarius Dwarf Galaxy** — ~26 kpc, currently merging with the Milky Way; tidal stream is famous
- **Magellanic Stream** — gas tail trailing the LMC/SMC; mostly invisible but iconic in the literature
- Other Local Group dwarfs (Draco, Ursa Minor, Sculptor, etc.) — small + faint; defer unless implementer finds they're worth including

Out of scope: M31 (Andromeda) at ~780 kpc — well beyond the model's typical volume; revisit only if we ever scale up.

**Implementation approach** (mirror `Milky Way wireframe` pattern):
- Centroid + ellipsoid (or appropriate primitive) per structure
- Wireframe mesh, low opacity, subtle by default
- Coordinate frame: published positions are typically Galactic — rotate to ICRS on ingest, same pattern as molecular clouds and Local Bubble

**Open questions to resolve during implementation:**
- **Labels?** TBD — for naked-eye targets like LMC/SMC, labels probably help (those fuzzy patches are recognisable in the sky and worth identifying). For dimmer structures, labels may be visual noise. Decide per-structure.
- **UI toggle:** single 'Satellite galaxies' toggle vs. per-structure toggles. Likely single is enough.

**Files likely touched:**
- New module `src/client/satellite-galaxies.ts` (or similar)
- Data inline in the module (small, no separate data file needed for a handful of structures)
- `src/client/controls.ts` — toggle
- `src/client/url-state.ts` — toggle persistence

**Future consideration (not a blocker):**
- Magellanic Clouds are naked-eye visible from southern latitudes — when they land, their chart-mode treatment should be evaluated against the m40 epic's table (`stellata-m40`). Probably add as outline glyphs in chart mode.

**No dependencies on other open issues.**

title: Chart-mode: solar-system planets at current t
description:
**Goal:** render the 8 planets in chart mode at their angular positions for the current `t`, using traditional planet symbols / labelling per `stellata-m40.1`.

**Implementation:**
- In chart mode, the planet positions from `stellata-3re.3` (VSOP87) project to angular sky coordinates
- Render as labelled symbols (♀ ♂ ♃ ♄ etc., or a filled disc + name), sized appropriately so they don't dominate adjacent star labels
- Time-aware: when the user scrubs `t`, planet positions update

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/chart-mode.ts`
- `src/client/chart-labels.ts`
- Possibly the solar-system rendering module from `stellata-3re.4` gets a chart-mode branch

**Visibility:** active when chart mode is engaged AND solar-system layer is active (Sol-focused or unfocused at Sol). Outside that, planets don't render in chart mode either.

**Depends on:** `stellata-m40.1` (symbology), `stellata-3re.4` (planet rendering ships first), `stellata-3re.3` (positions API).

title: Chart-mode: enlarge constellation name labels + increase transparency
description:
**Goal:** in chart mode, constellation name labels currently render small enough that individual letters can be occluded by adjacent star labels. Increase size by ~50% and increase transparency to compensate (so the larger labels don't dominate).

**Tweak target:**
- Size: ~50% larger
- Opacity: more transparent than today (since the larger size will compensate for legibility)

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/chart-labels.ts` — per-frame label engine + glyphs; constellation-name rendering lives here
- `src/client/chart-mode.ts` — orchestrator if any size/opacity uniforms live there
- Possibly `src/client/styles.css` if labels are SVG/HTML-based

**Verification:** load chart mode, check that overlapping constellation-name + star-label cases now read clearly without the constellation name dominating sparse regions of the sky.

title: Heliopause shell + scale rings (1 AU & 50 AU reference circles)
description:
**Goal:** render the heliopause as an asymmetric shell + faint reference rings at 1 AU and 50 AU centered on Sol. Provides spatial context for the inner solar system view.

**Heliopause:**
- Asymmetric shape — ~120 AU upwind (toward Galactic apex), ~200 AU downwind, with the recognised teardrop shape
- Procedural mesh (no source data needed for v1 — a simple parametric shell suffices)
- Faint translucent material, subtle by default

**Scale rings:**
- Faint circles at 1 AU (Earth orbit benchmark) and 50 AU (Kuiper belt edge / Pluto distance)
- In the ecliptic plane (ε rotated to ICRS, same as planet positions from `stellata-3re.3`)

**Visibility:** active only when solar-system layer is active (Sol-focused or unfocused at Sol).

**Independent of `t`** — geometry is static, no time dependence.

**Files likely touched:** the same `solar-system.ts` module from `stellata-3re.4` is the natural home.

title: Planet rendering: discs at physical angular size, orbit rings (1 AU & 50 AU)
description:
**Goal:** render the 8 planets at their per-frame ICRS positions as physically-sized discs. Leverages `stellata-a7d`'s rewrite for correct angular sizing.

**Disc sizing:** use `θ = 2·atan(R/d)` from `stellata-a7d.2`. Each planet has a known equatorial radius in AU (Earth = 4.26e-5 AU, Jupiter = 4.78e-4 AU, etc.); convert to parsecs.

**Implementation:**
- New rendering layer e.g. `src/client/solar-system.ts` and shader pair (or reuse star shaders if instance attributes can encode the difference cleanly)
- Reads `getPlanetPositions(t)` from `stellata-3re.3` per frame (or from the cache when `t` is unchanged)
- Visible only when solar-system layer is active (Sol-focused or unfocused at Sol)

**Out of scope:** lighting, surface texturing, atmospheric effects — discs only for v1.

**Depends on:** `stellata-3re.3` (positions) and benefits from `stellata-a7d.2` having landed (correct disc sizing).

title: VSOP87 planet positions via astronomia (ecliptic→ICRS, per-frame caching)
description:
**Goal:** compute heliocentric ICRS positions for the 8 planets at any `t` using VSOP87D coefficients via the `astronomia` npm package. No network calls; fully offline; ~500 KB added to bundle.

**Per-frame** (when solar-system layer is visible):
1. `JDE = tToJDE(t)` (helper from `stellata-3re.1`)
2. For each planet, call `astronomia.planetposition.Planet(...).position(JDE)` → `{ lon, lat, range }` heliocentric ecliptic, radians + AU
3. Cartesian:
   ```
   x_ecl = R cos(B) cos(L)
   y_ecl = R cos(B) sin(L)
   z_ecl = R sin(B)
   ```
4. Rotate ecliptic → ICRS equatorial (ε = 23.4392911° at J2000):
   ```
   x_eq = x_ecl
   y_eq = y_ecl cos(ε) − z_ecl sin(ε)
   z_eq = y_ecl sin(ε) + z_ecl cos(ε)
   ```
5. Convert AU → parsecs (`/ 206264.806`)

**Caching:** per-frame results cached, recomputed only when `t` changes.

**Output:** API `getPlanetPositions(t): { mercury: Vec3, venus: Vec3, …, neptune: Vec3 }` in ICRS parsecs. Consumed by `stellata-3re.4` (rendering).

**Depends on:** `stellata-3re.1` for `t` + JDE conversion.

title: Time-scrubber UI in .ui-bottom
description:
**Goal:** discreet time-scrubber affordance in `.ui-bottom` (next to the scale bar). Visible only when the solar-system layer is active (focused on Sol, or unfocused at Sol). Hidden otherwise.

**Brief:** unobtrusive, doesn't dominate the bottom bar when nothing solar-system is visible. Detailed widget choices deferred to implementation time.

**Likely shape:**
- Short text readout showing current `t` ('Live' when matching wall-clock within tolerance, otherwise a date)
- Clickable to open an edit affordance
- Forward / back nudges (e.g. ±1 day, ±1 year keys)

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/index.html`, `styles.css` for the chip
- `src/client/stellata.ts` for visibility wiring
- New module e.g. `src/client/time-scrubber.ts`

**Depends on:** `stellata-3re.1` for the `t` state + setter.

title: The 't' time variable: state, URL semantics, JD conversion
description:
**Goal:** introduce the wall-clock time variable `t` (Unix-seconds, double precision) that drives planet positions and probe trajectories. Foundational for the rest of the solar-system layer.

**State:**
- Lives on the stellata filter state alongside other filter fields
- Default at startup: `Date.now() / 1000`
- Public setter that triggers re-render of any time-dependent layers

**URL semantics:**
- URL state writes `t=<unix>` only when the user has scrubbed away from 'now'
- Absence in URL = 'live, defaults to current wall-clock at load time'
- Shared link with `t=` preserves sender's chosen date; without `t=` resolves to receiver's local-now
- This matches the share-link contract: deliberately scrubbed pose freezes time, idle pose stays live

**Conversion utilities:**
- `tToJDE(t: number): number` → `(t / 86400) + 2440587.5`
- Wall-clock-detection helper (within tolerance of `Date.now()` → 'Live')

**Out of scope:**
- The scrubber UI itself (`stellata-3re.2`)
- Variable-star animation continues using its independent `uTime` cosmetic clock — `t` and `uTime` are orthogonal

title: Rendering: orbiting planets, orbit rings, null-radius indicator
description:
**Goal:** when the user focuses a flagged host star, render its known exoplanets as orbiting bodies — orbital rings sized to `semi_major_axis_au`, planet discs sized to `radius_earth` / `radius_jupiter` where known.

**Visual decisions deferred to implementer** (see parent `stellata-bk5` for context):
- Orbit ring style (solid / dashed / fade-with-distance)
- Planet disc shading (flat / lit / temperature-tinted via `st_teff`)
- Null-radius indicator (an unknown-radius planet still has a known orbit; show the orbit ring + a default-size marker?)

**Leverage `stellata-a7d`:** disc sizing should use the same physical-angular-diameter formula `θ = 2·atan(R/d)` from the rewrite, not the legacy log-mapped formula. Planets at correct angular size at the camera distances expected when focused on a host star.

**Files touched:** likely a new `src/client/exoplanets.ts` rendering layer + shader pair, plus integration into `stellata.ts` focus path.

**Depends on:** `stellata-bk5.2` for data, and **strongly benefits from `stellata-a7d.2` having landed** so disc sizing is physically consistent.

title: Client loader: lazy-load exoplanets.json on first focus of a flagged host star
description:
**Goal:** when the user focuses a star whose 'has-exoplanets' flag bit is set, fetch `public/exoplanets.json` (once, cached for the session) and surface its `ExoplanetSystem` for the focused host.

**Implementation:**
- New `src/client/exoplanet-loader.ts` mirroring the structure of `catalog-loader.ts`
- Singleton fetch promise — first call kicks off the load, subsequent calls reuse the result
- API: `getExoplanetsForStar(idx: number): ExoplanetSystem | null`
- Wired into `stellata.ts` focus path: when a flagged star is focused, kick the fetch and (on resolve) update the rendering layer

**Out of scope:** any UI affordance is part of `stellata-bk5.3`. This issue ends at `getExoplanetsForStar()` returning data.

**Depends on:** `stellata-bk5.1` for the binary flag bit + json file.

title: Build pipeline: NASA Exoplanet Archive ingest, cross-match, catalog flag bit + lazy json
description:
**Goal:** ingest the NASA Exoplanet Archive `pscomppars` table at build time, cross-match host stars to ATHYG, set a 'has-exoplanets' flag bit per star in `catalog.bin`, and emit `public/exoplanets.json` keyed by host-star catalog index for lazy loading.

**Data fetch** (one-off, committed via Git LFS — see parent `stellata-bk5` for the exact curl URL and Git LFS commands):
- `data/exoplanets_nasa.csv` from the TAP API
- Fields: `pl_name, hostname, hip_name, hd_name, ra, dec, sy_dist, pl_rade, pl_radj, pl_orbsmax, pl_orbper, pl_eccen, pl_bmasse, sy_pnum, st_teff, st_rad, st_lum`

**Parser:** new `parseExoplanetData(csvPath)` alongside the GCVS parsers. Returns `Map<hostname, ExoplanetSystem>` with planets sorted by `semi_major_axis_au` ascending (nulls last). Schema in parent `stellata-bk5`. Don't filter null-radius planets — renderer handles that.

**Cross-match — HIP + HD + Gliese.** Build symmetric reverse maps (`exoByHip`, `exoByHd`, `exoByGl`) after the existing post-sort `hipToIndex` map. Resolve hostname → ATHYG catalog index; record on each matched star a 'has-exoplanets' bit + an offset into the lazy json.

**Outputs:**
- `catalog.bin` gains a flag bit per star (likely fits in an existing reserved bit; check the binary v4 layout)
- `public/exoplanets.json` keyed by catalog index → `ExoplanetSystem`. Lazy-loaded by the client on first focus of a star with the flag set.

**Files touched:** `scripts/build-catalog.ts`, `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` (new parser there per existing pattern), tests in `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts`.

title: Dataset boundary overlay: visualise where high-fidelity data ends (Edenhofer dust volume, etc.)
description:
**Frame:** part of the artificial-visualisation cluster — like the dust particle layer and MC silhouettes, this is an opt-in overlay that surfaces something you can't directly see in the rendered scene. Specifically *not* a "you're outside the realistic model" status chip: that framing implies degraded mode and breaks immersion. Going farther from the high-fidelity volume is just part of the experience; the underlying dataset boundary becomes visible only if the user opts in.

**What to visualise (initial scope):**
- The **Edenhofer dust voxel cube** (~1.25 kpc AABB at world origin) — the high-fidelity 3D dust volume that drives per-star extinction inside it. Outside, an analytical disc/scale-height profile takes over (see `stellata-74e`). Render as a faint translucent sphere or box mesh, off by default.

**Future extensions (not blockers for this issue):**
- ATHYG catalog completeness boundary (e.g. apparent-magnitude horizon as a faint shell)
- Zucker cloud coverage region
- Per-star dust extinction validity envelope
- Other dataset envelopes added later

**Implementation sketch:**
- Faint translucent mesh at world origin (size = Edenhofer AABB). Premultiplied alpha; very low opacity. Sits in the same toggle group as the rest of the artificial-viz overlays.
- No status chip, no banner, no "degraded" framing in the UI.

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/stellata.ts` — boundary mesh + group registration
- `src/client/controls.ts` — toggle in the artificial-viz panel section (once that section's UX is designed under `stellata-io3`)
- `src/client/url-state.ts` — toggle persistence
- `src/client/index.html`, `src/client/styles.css` — minimal

**Out of scope:**
- Any text indication that the user is in / out of a high-fidelity region — explicitly rejected during scoping.
- Changes to extinction physics — that's `stellata-74e`'s scope.

**Estimate:** ~2 hours once the artificial-viz toggle group exists.

**History:** extracted from `stellata-74e` Part 2 ("realism indicator + boundary mesh") on 2026-05-02. The original framing as a status chip was rejected; reframed as an artificial visualisation overlay alongside dust-particle viz and MC silhouettes.

title: Re-purpose shelved warm-glow MC overlay as part of artificial-viz toggle group
description:
**Goal:** the currently-shelved `cloud.frag.glsl` warm-glow MC overlay (decorative, not physical) is a candidate inhabitant of the artificial-visualisation layer. Decide its role *after* Epic A (`stellata-c7u`) lands physical MC rendering.

**Current state:** `FilterState.showMolecularClouds = false`, panel toggle removed, URL flag bit 2 reserved. Render path, shaders, and data are preserved.

**Question this issue captures:**
- After Epic A's physical MC rendering ships, do we *also* want the legacy artificial overlay as a separate user-toggleable layer? (Cases for: explicit "show me the cloud locations" mode for users who want to find clouds, similar in spirit to the existing molecular-cloud focus / vector / warp UX. Cases against: A.6's subtle stylised presence may already cover this need.)
- If yes: re-frame the warm-glow renderer as the artificial-viz cloud layer, distinct from Epic A's physical pass. Toggle lives in the artificial-viz group from `stellata-io3`.
- If no: delete the shelved code path entirely.

**Decision deferred** until Epic A's A.6 lands, so the team can evaluate whether subtle stylised presence is sufficient. This issue is a placeholder for that decision.

**Depends on Epic A's A.6 (`stellata-c7u.6`) for the assessment to make sense — relates_to dependency rather than blocks since neither outcome is required for the epic to ship.**

title: Artificial visualisation layer: structures invisible/near-invisible to the naked eye
description:
**Tracking epic — no decisions yet, just a grouping.**

**Frame:** some structures in the catalogue are essentially invisible (interstellar dust outside dense clouds), nearly invisible (most molecular clouds, against background stars), or only partially visible (faint nebulae, distant SF regions). Showing them at all is a *deliberate non-physical choice* — a visualisation, not a recreation. They belong together as a UX cluster: there should be a clear conceptual line between "what you'd actually see" and "what we're overlaying to help you see structure."

**Possible UX shape (deferred):**
- A single "artificial overlay" toggle group in the settings panel — e.g. dust visualisation, MC silhouettes, faint nebulae overlays — clearly labelled as visualisation, not as physical truth
- Each layer can be independently toggled within the group
- Off by default? On by default? — design decision later
- Possibly a single master switch with detail toggles underneath, or a mode-style switch (physical / visualisation / chart-mode)

**What this epic does NOT do today:** make any decisions. It tracks the related issues so a future design pass can treat them as one coherent UX cluster.

**Children (work issues that fit this frame):**
- `stellata-zq3` — Phase 2: Revisit dust visualisation layer (the always-artificial dust particle splat layer)
- (filed under this epic: a child to evaluate re-purposing the currently-shelved warm-glow MC silhouette as part of the artificial-viz toggle group, separate from Epic A's *physical* MC rendering)

**Related but not (yet) reparented:**
- `stellata-9hp` — Phase 3b: Nebulae catalogs (PN, RNe, H II, SNR). Many catalogue nebulae are faint enough to be effectively invisible without aperture; conceptually fits this frame, but it's a self-contained feature with its own scope. Revisit grouping if a unified "artificial overlay" UX emerges.
- `stellata-c7u` (Physical MC rendering epic) — the *physical* counterpart to the artificial MC overlay child below. Both can coexist; they answer different questions ("what would I see?" vs "where is the structure?").

**No hard dependencies; epic is purely organisational.**

title: A.7 Inside-the-cloud immersion + ambient response tuning
description:
**Goal:** confirm that flying into a cloud reads as physics. The expected experience (per scoping discussion):
- Stars whose line of sight traverses cloud material in front of the camera dim and redden proportional to remaining-depth.
- Peripheral stars (off-axis, sightline doesn't traverse cloud) are unaffected.
- Ambient gets very dark — there's almost no scattered light inside an MC in the visible.
- Where embedded young stars carve cavities (A.5), local density drops and visibility recovers near them.

**This phase is mostly tuning + verification once A.2–A.6 have landed:**
- Verify per-star extinction integration handles the camera-inside-cloud case correctly (the raymarch starts inside the medium; integration must work from camera→star regardless of which side of the envelope the camera is on).
- Tune ambient / fog so darkness is conveyed without losing the sky entirely (some fraction of distant starfield should remain visible — the sky outside the cloud should not be pitch-black if the cloud is finite).
- Test all known approachable clouds: Taurus, Orion A, Orion B, Perseus, Ophiuchus, Chamaeleon, Lupus, Cepheus, Pipe, Corona Australis. Record any cloud-specific calibration tweaks needed.

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/stellata.ts` — ambient / fog adjustments per camera position relative to cloud
- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — verify camera-inside-medium raymarch correctness
- Possibly `docs/molecular-clouds.md` for the cloud-by-cloud notes

**Visual gate:** flying into Taurus produces a dimming/reddening experience that feels physical — not a hard fog wall, not unchanged starfield.

**Depends on A.2–A.6.**

title: A.6 Subtle stylised 'presence' pass for empty-sky cloud silhouette
description:
**Goal:** physical extinction is the dominant signal, but on directions where there are no bright background stars the cloud would be invisible. A *very* subtle stylised pass conveys the cloud's shape without competing with the physical extinction signal.

**Constraints:**
- Must read as decorative, not as the cloud's body. Users should never mistake the stylised hint for the actual luminous emission of the cloud (MCs barely emit in visible).
- Easy to under-do; easy to over-do. Tune so the stylisation is invisible against starfields and only emerges over starless directions.
- Should respect mode: chart mode already has its own isobar contour treatment (`uChartIsobar` path in `cloud.frag.glsl`); coordinate so the stylised presence is dark-mode-only or differs between modes.

**Possible approaches (decision in A.1):**
- Re-purpose the existing shelved `cloud.frag.glsl` warm-glow as the stylised pass, but with opacity ~0.05–0.1 instead of decorative levels, possibly modulated by absence of background stars
- Faint fresnel-style edge glow keyed to silhouette direction
- Substructure noise (from A.4) reading through as faint texture rather than smooth shading

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/shaders/cloud.frag.glsl` — possibly retained, possibly rewritten
- `src/client/molecular-clouds.ts` — re-enables the layer (`FilterState.showMolecularClouds` default flip)
- `src/client/controls.ts` — re-add the panel toggle if we want one (decision in A.1)
- `src/client/url-state.ts` — URL flag bit 2 was reserved; re-encode or leave reserved per A.1 decision

**Visual gate:** flying past Taurus against a starfield reads as physical (stars dim and redden behind it). Flying past Taurus against a star-poor direction reads as a *very faint* hint of cloud shape, never as a luminous blob.

**Depends on A.3, A.4 ideally landed first so the physical signal exists before the decorative pass is layered on top.**

title: A.5 Embedded young-star cavities + cloud-type variations (HII, reflection nebulosity)
description:
**Goal:** model the cloud-shaping effect of embedded young stars. O/B stars carve density cavities via stellar winds + ionising radiation (Strömgren spheres, HII regions), and hot enough hosts produce visible emission. This is what gives an active SF region (Orion A) a different inside-the-cloud experience from a quiescent dark cloud (Taurus dark cores).

**Cross-match (build time):**
- Identify O/B stars and known YSOs whose 3D positions fall inside any Zucker cloud bbox
- Use ATHYG spectral types for O/B identification
- For each cloud, list embedded hot stars with: position, spectral type, luminosity proxy
- Output: per-cloud cavity descriptor in the dust manifest or cloud JSON

**Density modulation:**
- For each embedded hot star, reduce local density inside a radius scaled by stellar luminosity (Strömgren-sphere-style; constants from A.1)
- Soft falloff at the cavity edge — `density × (1 − cavity_strength × falloff(r/r_cav))`

**Cloud-type variation:**
- Quiescent dark cloud (no/few hot embeddings): high peak density, sharp envelope
- Active SF region (multiple hot embeddings): cavities, lower mean density, optionally an emission overlay
- HII region: when embedded star is hot enough (≥ early B), add a faint emission/scattering term — a red/pink tinge near the star, fading with distance. Threshold from A.1.

**Reflection nebulosity (optional within scope):** B-type stars in dusty regions backscatter blue light. If included, decide whether it's worth modelling here or deferred to the Phase 3b Nebulae epic (`stellata-9hp`) which already covers reflection nebulae as discrete blob objects. Likely defer.

**Visual gate:** Orion A (multiple O stars) should render visibly differently from Taurus dark-cloud cores (quiescent) — Orion's density should feel "hollowed", and the area around hot embedded stars should be perceptibly less dim.

**Depends on A.2 (data pipeline) and A.4 (substructure landscape into which cavities are carved).**

title: A.4 Procedural substructure: 3D noise inside cloud envelope for filamentary density
description:
**Goal:** real MCs are filamentary and clumpy with a log-normal/power-law density PDF, not smooth ellipsoids. Substructure makes the cloud feel real — both visually (where the subtle stylised pass A.6 reads filaments) and physically (extinction varies along sightlines through the cloud, not just by depth).

**Approach:** procedural 3D noise (fBm and/or Worley/Voronoi) shaped by the ellipsoid envelope from Zucker. Octaves and amplitudes calibrated in A.1. The noise modulates density inside the envelope; outside the envelope density goes to zero smoothly.

**Where the noise is sampled — design decision in A.1:**
- (a) Bake into the dust voxel grid in `build-dust.py` — single source of truth, but voxel resolution caps detail.
- (b) Evaluate per-fragment in `star.vert.glsl` raymarch — finer detail, but more shader cost per star and harder to integrate.
- (c) Hybrid: voxel-scale noise baked in, sub-voxel detail evaluated in shader for the close-camera case.

**Implementation depends on (a/b/c):**
- (a) → `scripts/build-dust.py`
- (b) → `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` plus a noise function include
- (c) → both

**Visual gate:** a sightline grazing the edge of Taurus should show patchy/filamentary extinction (not a smooth ellipsoid darkening), and the subtle stylised presence pass (A.6) should read structured, not blob-like.

**Depends on A.2.**

title: A.3 Wavelength-selective extinction: redden background stars passing through dust
description:
**Goal:** the per-star extinction layer in `star.vert.glsl` should redden background stars, not just dim them. This is the dominant *visible* signature of an MC against rich star fields and is what makes a dust lane look like a dust lane.

**Investigate first:** does the current per-star extinction path apply a single grey magnitude penalty, or does it already separate B/R? Confirm during research and adjust scope accordingly. (A.1 should answer this; record findings here.)

**Model:** standard interstellar reddening with RV = A_V / E(B−V) ≈ 3.1 (Cardelli-Clayton-Mathis 1989). Compute E(B−V) per star from the integrated optical depth, then apply A_B = A_V × (RV+1)/RV and A_R proportionally. Use existing per-star B and V (or B-V) channels in the catalog binary.

**Implementation:**
- Touch: `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl`, possibly `star.frag.glsl`
- Add a reddening factor in the colour pipeline so the displayed star colour shifts toward red proportional to extinction depth, on top of the existing dimming
- Verify chart-mode and observer mode both pick up the reddening (or one of them is intentionally exempt — decide in A.1)

**Visual gate:** an O/B star (intrinsically blue) sitting behind ~10 mag of MC dust should render distinctly red, not just dim blue.

**Depends on A.2** for the calibrated dust density. Doesn't depend on A.4 (substructure) or A.5 (cavities) — those refine the density field A.3 reads from.

title: A.2 Build pipeline: bake Zucker MC density into Edenhofer dust voxel volume
description:
**Goal:** make the per-star extinction raymarch in `star.vert.glsl` the single source of truth for absorption, with Zucker MCs supplying higher-fidelity local density inside the Edenhofer volume. No double-counting between the two layers.

**Touches:**
- `scripts/build-dust.py` — at the end of the existing dust-volume bake, overlay Zucker MC contributions per voxel. Each cloud's density profile (centroid, semi-axes, density model from A.1) replaces or augments the underlying Edenhofer value where they overlap.
- `data/dust/manifest.json` — bump version; record that Zucker has been merged in.
- `scripts/build-clouds.py` — likely needs to emit the per-cloud parameters consumed by `build-dust.py` (peak density, profile shape, etc.) in addition to today's bbox/centroid.

**Decision points (resolved in A.1, applied here):**
- Replace vs additive blend in overlap voxels
- Whether procedural substructure (A.4) is baked into the voxel field at build time, or applied per-fragment in the shader at runtime, or both
- Calibration: ensure that flying through Taurus produces reasonable Av (~5–15+ mag) rather than under- or over-extinction

**Sanity gate:** `scripts/verify-catalog.ts`-equivalent check for the dust manifest — pick a cloud (e.g. Taurus), compute a sightline through its centre, confirm Av matches the literature value within a factor of ~2.

**Out of scope:** the shader-side reddening (A.3) and substructure noise (A.4) are downstream. This phase produces the data they consume.

title: Fullscreen mode + optional 'hide chrome' toggle
description:
Two related affordances filed in one issue because they both serve the same end (immersive / presentation / screenshot use):

1. Browser fullscreen — wire the Fullscreen API to a button in the brand box (or settings panel — decide during implementation) and a keyboard shortcut. Toggling enters/exits fullscreen on the canvas container so the panel and overlays are still visible inside fullscreen by default.

2. Hide chrome — a separate toggle that hides UI chrome (panel, top bar, overlays except the canvas itself + maybe scale bar) so the user can grab a clean shot or present without the UI. This works independently of fullscreen — you can hide chrome without going fullscreen, and vice versa.

Critical files:
- src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts — Esc progression at line 184 (must precede the existing observe→navigate fallback when fullscreen is active); add the new shortcut(s) here.
- src/client/help-modal.ts — must list both shortcuts.
- src/client/index.html, src/client/styles.css — chrome targets (panel container, brand box, top-bar, overlays). A single body[data-chrome-hidden] toggle is the cleanest selector hook; confirm during implementation that the existing layout containers cooperate.
- src/client/url-state.ts — natural home for state persistence (single bit each in the URL flag set). Skip persistence if it causes a confusing first-load (user lands on a page with no UI).
- src/client/brand-modal.ts (or controls.ts) — wherever the user-visible toggle button lands.

UX:
- Pressing Esc exits fullscreen first (if active), then falls through to the existing Esc progression in keyboard-shortcuts.ts:184.
- "Hide chrome" should be reversible by the same shortcut/control; show a small unobtrusive "show UI" affordance (corner button or bottom hint) so users aren't trapped.

Verify before close:
- Fullscreen toggle works in Chrome, Safari, Firefox.
- On exit, layout returns exactly as it was.
- Hide-chrome restores cleanly via shortcut and via the visible "show UI" affordance.
- Help modal lists both shortcuts.
- npm run typecheck and npm test pass.

Out of scope: any kiosk/auto-hide-on-idle behaviour, mobile-specific chrome rules (those land under the mobile epic).

title: 'F' shortcut: find a star and point camera at it (no warp)
description:
Add a keyboard shortcut F that opens the same search modal used by G, but instead of warping the camera to the chosen star, only orients the camera at it.

Behaviour by mode:
- Observe: rotate the camera (yaw + pitch) to point at the selected star. Animate the rotation rather than snap. FOV unchanged. Camera position unchanged.
- Navigate: orient the orbit gaze toward the star without travelling — i.e. rotate the trackball target so the star sits at the centre of the view, but do NOT warp the camera position to it (that's G's job) and do NOT mark it as focused (no focus-ring, no disc-mask). The animation should match observe-mode feel.

Critical files & reuses:
- src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts — existing G binding at line 89; add the F case adjacent. Modal-open + editable-target guards already in place around line 80.
- goModal (instantiated in keyboard-shortcuts.ts) — confirm during implementation that it can be parameterised by intent ("warp" vs "look") rather than duplicated.
- src/client/search.ts — the search/typeahead behind the modal.
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — yaw/pitch source for the observe rotation animation.
- src/client/stellata.ts — trackball target / orbit gaze; the navigate-mode "swing target without travel" lives here.
- src/client/help-modal.ts — must be updated to list F.

UX:
- Pressing F while a modal is already open is a no-op (existing modal-open guard handles this).
- Esc cancels the search modal as today.

Verify before close:
- In observe, F → pick a star → camera smoothly rotates to it.
- In navigate, F → pick a star → orbit gaze swings to it; camera position unchanged; no focus indicators activate.
- npm run typecheck and npm test pass.
- Help modal shows F.

Out of scope: changing what G does. Adding F to the panel as a button (keyboard-only is fine for v1).

title: Deep-space probes
description:
**Status:** Depends on Phase 12a's time-scrubber framework.

**Goal:** render the five interstellar probes — Pioneer 10, Pioneer 11, Voyager 1, Voyager 2, New Horizons — at their actual positions for the current `t`. Visible only when solar-system layer is active. A small but emotionally significant addition: "you can see how far they've gotten" against the scale of the solar system and the nearest stars.

### Probe data via NASA HORIZONS

| Probe        | Launch     | HORIZONS ID | Status                |
|--------------|------------|-------------|-----------------------|
| Pioneer 10   | 1972-03-03 | `-23`       | Silent since 2003-01-23 |
| Pioneer 11   | 1973-04-06 | `-24`       | Silent since 1995-09-30 |
| Voyager 2    | 1977-08-20 | `-32`       | Active                |
| Voyager 1    | 1977-09-05 | `-31`       | Active                |
| New Horizons | 2006-01-19 | `-98`       | Active                |

### Build script: `scripts/fetch-probe-trajectories.ts`

**Manual** — `npm run fetch:probes` only, not part of the default `build` chain. Same convention as the GCVS files (committed once, refreshed when upstream updates).

Per probe, query HORIZONS:
```
https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/api/horizons.api?format=json
  &COMMAND=<id>
  &CENTER=500@10
  &MAKE_EPHEM=YES
  &EPHEM_TYPE=VECTORS
  &VEC_TABLE=2
  &REF_PLANE=FRAME
  &START_TIME=<launch date>
  &STOP_TIME=2050-01-01
  &STEP_SIZE=30d
```

`REF_PLANE=FRAME` returns ICRS equatorial directly — same frame as ATHYG and the rest of the app. No ecliptic rotation needed.

Parse the `$$SOE…$$EOE` block, extract `JDTDB` and `X, Y, Z` (in AU) per step. Output `data/probes/{id}.json`:
```json
{
  "id": "voyager1",
  "label": "Voyager 1",
  "launchDate": "1977-09-05",
  "launchUnix": 242265600,
  "lastContactUnix": null,
  "trajectory": [
    { "jd": 2443413.5, "x": 0.12, "y": -0.03, "z": 0.01 },
    ...
  ]
}
```

`lastContactUnix` is null for active probes, populated for Pioneer 10 (`2003-01-23`) and Pioneer 11 (`1995-09-30`).

30-day step from launch to 2050: ~880 entries × ~50 bytes = ~45 KB per probe, ~225 KB total for 5 probes. Plain-text commit, no LFS.

### Sync to runtime

Mirror `data/probes/*.json` → `public/probes/*.json` via a small `scripts/sync-probes.ts` (same pattern as `sync-dust.ts`). Add to `build` and `dev` script chains. `public/probes/` gitignored.

### Runtime: `src/client/probe-loader.ts`

- Fetch all 5 JSONs in parallel (small enough — fire-and-forget at startup or lazy-load on first solar-system-layer activation).
- For a given `t`, find bracketing entries and **linearly interpolate** x/y/z. Probes move ~3–4 AU/year — 30-day samples are visually indistinguishable from the true smooth trajectory.
- Hide probe entirely if `t < launchUnix`.
- Visual flag for "extrapolated past last-contact": Pioneer 10 after 2003-01-23, Pioneer 11 after 1995-09-30. Render dimmer, with a "(signal lost)" label suffix.

### Probe rendering

Same instanced-quad pattern as planets. Tiny — a few px. Always-labelled when visible (probes are a discovery feature, the labels are the point). Optional: a thin fading trail showing the trajectory between launch and current `t`.

### Files

- New:
  - `scripts/fetch-probe-trajectories.ts` — manual HORIZONS fetcher.
  - `scripts/sync-probes.ts` — `data/probes/` → `public/probes/`.
  - `src/client/probe-loader.ts` — JSON load + interpolation.
  - `data/probes/{voyager1,voyager2,pioneer10,pioneer11,new-horizons}.json` — committed (no LFS).
- Touch:
  - `package.json` — `fetch:probes` script + `sync-probes` in chain.
  - `solar-system.ts` (from 12a) — register probe layer alongside planets, share label/render plumbing where sensible.
  - `.gitignore` — `/public/probes/`.
- After landing, extend the `CLAUDE.md` "Solar-system layer" section with the probe data pipeline and last-contact handling.

### Watch out for

- HORIZONS rate-limits and occasional schema drift. Fetch script should be defensive — clear error if `$$SOE` block is missing or columns are unexpected.
- Pre-launch hiding is per-probe based on `launchUnix`. Don't accidentally render a probe at `t = 0` (1970-01-01) where its trajectory array's first entry would extrapolate backward.
- Linear interpolation between widely-spaced 30-day samples is fine for visual purposes but should not be billed as "true ephemeris." Comment in code so future-you doesn't mistake it for a precision ephemeris API.
- Active probes' projected trajectories past *now* are HORIZONS forward-projections, not certainties. The data is correct as far as projections go but the wording in any tooltips should be careful.
- Annual-ish refresh: re-run `npm run fetch:probes`, commit the refreshed JSONs. Document the cadence in CLAUDE.md.

### Estimate

Half a day to a day. Fetch script is the bulk; runtime interpolation + rendering is small once 12a's planet rendering is in place.

### Deliberately excluded

- Other interstellar probes / spacecraft (Parker Solar Probe, JUICE, etc.) — not yet at solar-system-edge scale where they'd be visually meaningful in this layer.
- True ephemeris precision (sub-AU). Linear interpolation between monthly samples is good enough for visualisation.
- Trajectory-line rendering past current `t` for active probes. Trail is launch-to-now only; future projection isn't shown (would need clearer "this is a projection" framing in UI).

title: Solar-system layer (time + planets + heliopause + scale rings)
description:
**Status:** Standalone. Introduces the wall-clock time scrubber that Phase 12b builds on.

**Goal:** when focused on Sol (or unfocused at Sol), render the local solar-system context: the 8 planets in their actual at-time positions, the heliopause boundary as an asymmetric shell, and faint reference rings at 1 AU and 50 AU. All driven by a new `t` time variable that defaults to the current wall-clock time and is user-scrubbable.

This is the first feature where the app cares about *actual* dates. Existing variable-star animation continues to use its independent cosmetic clock — `uTime` and the new `t` are orthogonal.

### The `t` time variable

Unix-seconds, double precision. Lives on the stellata filter state alongside other filter fields. Default is `Date.now() / 1000` evaluated at startup. URL state writes `t=<unix>` only when the user has scrubbed away from "now"; absence in URL = "live, defaults to current wall-clock at load time."

URL semantics intent: a shared link with `t=` preserves the sender's chosen date; a shared link without `t=` reads as "live", so it resolves to the receiver's local-now. This matches how shareable links should feel — a deliberately scrubbed pose freezes time, an idle pose stays live.

### Time-scrubber UI

Discreet. Lives in `.ui-bottom`. Broad idea: a short text readout showing the current `t` ("Live" when matching wall-clock within tolerance, otherwise a date), clickable to open an edit affordance. **Detailed widget choices deferred to implementation time** — the brief is "unobtrusive, doesn't dominate the bottom bar when nothing solar-system is visible."

Visible only when the solar-system layer is active (focused on Sol, or unfocused at Sol). Hidden otherwise.

### Sol-focus `minDistance` relaxation

When focused on Sol, drop `controls.minDistance` from `0.005 pc` (≈1031 AU) down to `~0.5 AU` ≈ `2.4e-6 pc`, so the user can fly into the inner solar system and resolve individual planets. Other focal stars retain the existing `0.005 pc` floor.

This is safe specifically for Sol because Sol sits at the world origin — the float32 jitter that bites at small distances *from non-origin focal stars* (CLAUDE.md "Camera near plane vs controls minDistance") doesn't apply when the focal frame is also the world frame.

`camera.near` already at `0.001 pc` is fine; for Sol-focus consider dropping to `1e-7 pc` (≈0.02 AU) so very close planet inspection isn't culled. Keep the strict-less-than invariant (`camera.near < minDistance`).

### Planet positions via VSOP87 (`astronomia` npm)

Add `astronomia` as a dependency. Provides full VSOP87D coefficient tables for all 8 planets with a clean JS API — no network calls, fully offline, ~500 KB added to bundle (acceptable; the data is the cost, not the algorithm).

Per-frame (when solar-system layer is visible):
1. `JDE = (t / 86400) + 2440587.5`
2. For each planet, call `astronomia.planetposition.Planet(...).position(JDE)` → `{ lon, lat, range }` heliocentric ecliptic, radians + AU.
3. Cartesian:
   ```
   x_ecl = R cos(B) cos(L)
   y_ecl = R cos(B) sin(L)
   z_ecl = R sin(B)
   ```
4. Rotate ecliptic → ICRS equatorial (ε = 23.4392911° at J2000):
   ```
   x_eq = x_ecl
   y_eq = y_ecl cos(ε) − z_ecl sin(ε)
   z_eq = y_ecl sin(ε) + z_ecl cos(ε)
   ```
5. Convert AU → parsecs (`/ 206264.806`) for the 3D scene.

Caching: VSOP87 evaluation is cheap but not free; cache results per-frame, recompute only when `t` changes.

### Planet rendering

Each planet as an instanced quad (similar to the star quads, separate `InstancedBufferGeometry`). Apparent-size with floor — physical AU sizes are tiny relative to orbital scale, so use `max(physicalRadius, minPx)` in the vertex shader. Body colour from a small per-planet constant table (Mercury grey, Mars rust, Jupiter banded yellow, etc.). Optional: a faint ring/label sphere texture at higher zoom.

Faint dashed orbit ellipses rendered as Three.js `Line` instances — one per planet, computed once from the planet's mean orbital elements (use VSOP87 at twelve evenly-spaced mean anomalies and connect, or use Keplerian elements directly). Re-renders only on UI-toggle, not per frame.

DOM labels for each planet — extend the existing `meta` overlay pattern or add a small `solar-system-labels.ts` module that does project-and-place per frame.

### Heliopause — asymmetric ellipsoid

Best-known shape per IBEX observations + Voyager 1/2 crossing data:
- **Apex (upwind, toward solar apex):** ~122 AU. Voyager 1 crossed at ~121 AU on 2012-08-25.
- **Flanks (perpendicular to apex direction):** ~115 AU. Voyager 2 crossed at ~119 AU on 2018-11-05 at ~67° from apex.
- **Heliotail (downwind):** ~200 AU. Recent IBEX/Cassini ENA data + global heliospheric models suggest 200–350 AU; the tail is the least-constrained direction. 200 AU is a defensible mid-estimate.

Apex direction: solar apex of motion through the local interstellar medium. ICRS approx **RA 17h53m, Dec +27.4°** (Frisch & Slavin 2013 LISM inflow direction). Document the source in code so future sessions don't ad-hoc this.

**Implementation as a clean ellipsoid + offset:**
- Total length along apex–tail axis = 122 + 200 = 322 AU.
- Major semi-axis (along apex–tail) = 161 AU.
- Equatorial semi-axes (in flank plane) = 115 AU.
- Centre of ellipsoid offset from Sol by (200 − 122)/2 = 39 AU toward the heliotail (anti-apex). Result: upwind boundary lands at 122 AU from Sol, tail boundary at 200 AU.

Construction: `SphereGeometry(1, 64, 32)` → non-uniform scale `(115, 115, 161)` along its local axes → orient so local +Z is the anti-apex direction → translate by 39 AU along that direction. Convert AU → pc for the scene.

Material: low-opacity additive shell (transparent, `depthWrite=false`), or a wireframe mesh. Pick one and tune. Label "Heliopause (~120 AU upwind)" — the asymmetry is intentional and the label should acknowledge it without over-specifying the tail.

Static — no `t` dependence (heliopause shape doesn't measurably change on human timescales).

### Scale rings — 1 AU and 50 AU

Faint `LineLoop` circles in the ICRS xy-plane (close enough to the ecliptic for visual reference; not the ecliptic plane proper, which is tilted 23.4°). Scale anchors:
- **1 AU** — Earth's orbit, inner-system context.
- **50 AU** — Kuiper Belt outer edge, outer-system context.

Visible only when solar-system layer is active. Optional toggle in the panel (default on).

### Files

- New:
  - `src/client/solar-system.ts` — VSOP87 driver, planet rendering, label placement, ring meshes.
  - `src/client/heliopause.ts` — static asymmetric mesh.
  - `src/client/time-scrubber.ts` — discreet UI in `.ui-bottom`.
- Touch:
  - `stellata.ts` — filter-state `t` field, Sol-focus `minDistance` swap, solar-system layer hookup.
  - `controls.ts` — possibly a panel toggle for orbits/rings.
  - `url-state.ts` — `t=` parameter (omit when "live").
  - `index.html` / `styles.css` — time-scrubber container in `.ui-bottom`, ring/orbit visibility classes.
  - `package.json` — add `astronomia` dependency.
- After landing, update `CLAUDE.md`:
  - Add a "Solar-system layer" section (VSOP87 coordinate transform chain, Sol-focus minDistance carve-out, t vs uTime distinction, heliopause apex direction reference).
  - Note in "Things deliberately kept out": time-series proper motion of stars is *still* out of scope — `t` only affects solar-system bodies.

### Watch out for

- **Sol-focus `minDistance` is a per-focus override.** When focus switches from Sol to another star, the floor must snap back to `0.005 pc` *before* the new focus's recenter pulls the camera in. `setFocus` is the right hook.
- **`t` vs `uTime`.** Don't accidentally couple them. `uTime` keeps ticking at `uSecondsPerDay = 0.2` regardless of where the scrubber is. Variable-star pulsation is cosmetic; mixing in wall-clock would either freeze pulsation when scrubbed or produce nonsensical strobing when the scrubber moves fast.
- **Ecliptic ↔ equatorial obliquity.** Use J2000 ε = 23.4392911° consistently. Don't reach for the time-varying obliquity term unless someone proves it makes a visible difference (it doesn't on visualisation timescales).
- **Solar-system layer is only meaningful from Sol-focus.** If you render planets from a Tau Ceti view they'd be sub-pixel close to Sol anyway, but explicit gating avoids edge cases (e.g. orbit ellipse meshes that shouldn't render at all when not at Sol).
- **`camera.near` invariant.** Anything that drops `minDistance` must keep `camera.near < minDistance`. Trivial but easy to miss when both shift together for Sol-focus.

### Estimate

1.5–2 days. VSOP87 wiring is mechanical (the library does the heavy lifting), heliopause mesh + scale rings are quick, time-scrubber UI is the most variable bit. Most time goes to making planet rendering look good across the new much-wider zoom range.

### Deliberately excluded

- Moons (Earth's Moon, Galilean satellites, Titan, etc.). VSOP87 doesn't cover these and the data plumbing for satellite ephemerides is a separate effort.
- Asteroids and minor planets. Out of scope for v1.
- Time-series proper motion of stars. Stars remain snapshot-only; `t` is solar-system-only.
- Time-of-day / Earth rotation effects. The scene is heliocentric; Earth's daily rotation has no meaning here.
- Real-time mode that animates `t` automatically forward. The scrubber is for explicit positioning; "Live" mode resolves to the current wall-clock at frame time but doesn't sweep.
